body: I've worked with Amazon reps twice now to try to fix a recurring problem with the FireTV stick I purchased. All they can do apparently is a temporary fix. It fails again in a day or less after resetting to factory defaults (the supposed fix). While streaming TV shows (I use Hulu plus Live mostly) the picture will freeze, appear to be buffering and, after a minute or two, give me an error pop up indicating "there was trouble accessing this material". Sometimes it advises me there is no internet access and I should reset my router or modem. I'm sure its not the network as all other devices continue to work fine, and I can even go to the streaming service that locked up on the FireTV stick and see what I was watching perfectly well, even while the FireTV still appears to be buffering and indicates that the service cannot be accessed. I have to reset the firetv stick to get it going again. The last time I chatted with Amazon reps, I asked for a replacement. All I got was a change of subject, indicating that "(they) understand my frustration and that we will get it fixed" (nice script). For years I've had mostly good experiences with Amazon, but with this one issue its been a nightmare. So, I'm moving on to something other than FireTV as I prefer to actually watch TV rather than having to reset it every day, sometimes hourly, to get it going. (I did not rate Voice Recognition or Alexa Integration as I don't use either.)

title: 1 Year Review: Upgrade to the 4K stick if at all possible!
body: Here's the quick takeaway: Absolutely purchase the FireTV 4K if you can spare the minor extra cost, because the upgraded processor is definitely worth it! Over the course of a year of use, the device has gotten NOTICEABLY SLOWER, and even after doing a factory reset on the device, it's still not nearly as snappy as the FireTV 4K stick that we have on our main TV.I've owned every version of the FireTV (minus the recast) from version 1 to the current 4K stick, and all of the devices, except the 4K stick have slowed over time and even factory resets don't seem to impact it's performance much. To this day the 4K is snappy every time. It's definitely worth the few extra bucks!Comparing the two devices, the features are very similar, except for 4K and HDR support on the higher-end model, and support for Dolby Vision. But the internals of the device are big improvements for performance:Upgraded, more powerful processor2GB of RAM vs. 1GB for the standard stickNOTE: The FireTV 4K absolutely DOES work on normal 1080p televisions. Other comments and even an Amazon rep had said that it only works on 4K televisions, but this is WRONG! You'll get the highest resolution that your TV allows, but only the 4K can output: 4K resolution.Overall, there's little reason not to spend the extra few dollars to get the upgraded device and the experience is so worth it!

title: Unreliable and doesn't compare to Roku
body: I have two Fire Sticks in my house. One I acquired from someone else, the other was free with some kinda sign up (sling TV maybe). Anyway, the first mentioned doesn't work anymore. It's about 3 years old and the apps freeze up often, lately Hulu and ATT Watch Now. Sometimes Netflix. There's no sound or I'm waiting forever for something to load. I've restarted, reset, cleared caches, cleared data and I always end up with something not working correctly. The remote died and I had to use my phone. I gave up on that one and went back to my Roku (that literally has none of these issues). My second Fire Stick is newer. About a year and a half since I got it. It has yet to have sound issues but the apps still have problems. As I said, the remotes aren't great so I switch to my phone app for it. Even that will have issues. I sometimes have to disconnect from my wifi and reconnect just to control the stick.Basically, I can only compare these to my Rokus. Now that Roku has a Prime video app, I see no reason to keep the stick around. The Roku prior to my current lasted 6 years before it started getting laggy and weird. I gave it away and it's still being used.All in all, compared to a Roku, the Fire Stick feels like a modded device - like I jailbroke my phone and only some apps work properly, others with bugs or not at all. It doesn't function like a device from a billion dollar company.

title: Pretty disappointed after the first week
body: All the buzz on streaming video and talk of cutting the cable got me to purchase a pair of Firesticks for our televisions. After living with them for a week now, I'm pretty underwhelmed.First of all, we only get two of the 6 or 7 local stations we liked, requiring the addition of an over the air antenna, and even with the antenna we can't get one of our favorite local programs.Second, we have to constantly search for the cable channels and programs we used to enjoy, it seems that every day they are on a different feed. Sometimes a feed will drop out in the middle of a program requiring a search for another.Third, picture quality is awful. Almost everything is in 720p, even the "HD" channels. There is no crispness to the video. Watching live sports like hockey, the video looks like the game was filmed and we are watching a movie. On some channels, it looks like it's cycling between 720 and 480!Last, the buffering can be very annoying. At times it is almost constant, stopping the feed as often as a couple times every minute. Really sucks when you're watching a sports event. When watching a game, we find ourselves spending a lot of time looking for a better feed, either you miss a lot because of the buffering, or the time you spend looking for a better feed.Cable is beginning to look like not so bad of a deal.

title: Don't buy it outside US
body: Everyday I connect my laptop in my TV, open Prime Video and enjoy my favorite TV Show with my wife.After a long time I take a decision and buy the FireTv and received yesterday. I setup all the system and work really good with Netflix, Youtube and other app, and honestly I love the quick response between the control and the Stick but is impossible reproduce any video from Prime Video using my FireTv, yesterday the support service solved the issue but a soon I open the Prime Video page in my computer all the system have the same issue. So I can not spend 5/10 minute chatting with Support Service everytime that I want to see my Tv Shows.I keep the stick and use for Netflix, Youtube, HBO but is really crazy that I bought this FireTv to use my Prime Video and is the only app that I can't use. SO If I want to use my Prime Video I'll connect my laptop in my TV like the old time.Just to let you know, the error that I got is 5055 and means your location is wrong and other error is "Home is not available in this moment, impossible to connect with our server" if you have this two errors try to chat with Support service and they will solve it.Control is awesome.WIFI Connection awesome.Other app's works perfect.Voice control is OK, but I have alexa in all my house so I don't use the voice control too much.

title: Sucks
body: This product is horrible. You’re literally taking a gamble as to whether or not you receive a fire stick that’s overheating or one that’s working properly. I ordered a fire stick in July 2019 and it has been overheating from the get go. What happens is the fire stick will keep restarting and you never get to watch anything. I contacted Amazon and they sent me a slip to return the product. The product never got to them because UPS somehow dropped the ball and lost my package. Amazon and their representatives Decided to refund me the amount that I purchased the stick for, which was half the price because I bought it on prime day. They also gave me a $25 gift card. The problem I have with this whole process is that Amazon is well aware that these fire sticks are overheating and they refuse to do anything about it; this has been going on with previous versions as well and their engineers or whom ever keep sending these fire sticks out when they know that they’re not functioning to their full capacity. Amazon can do better! So frustrated and disappointed. Here’s the sucky part, I will still go ahead and buy another fire stick and who knows I may get a dud again or I may get one that’s working well. I will say this though if I get another dud I am done with Amazon because they are lackadaisical and seem to take their consumers for granted.

title: ALEXA AND THE FIRE STICK ARE NOT SO BRILLIANT a.k.a. FIRE STICK I FART IN YOUR GENERAL DIRECTION
body: I want everything back the way that it was, which means the Amazon app works on my smart TV and I can summon Netflix with the red button on my remote control, but no. Having everything working right was too good to be true. Amazon forced me to buy a fire stick by discontinuing its support for the app on my smart TV. Here's what annoys me: 1. I use the fire stick to turn on Amazon Prime. My list of movies I want to watch has vanished. 2. I can no longer access Netflix with the Netflix button on my remote control. I don't know why the fire stick made the remote control Netflix button quit working, but it did. As with Amazon movies, I can no longer see my Netflix list of movies that I want to watch. 3. I tried to watch a documentary series on Prime. Every time I needed to pause the show, I'd return to find that it had disappeared. I'd have to search for it and then it wouldn't start playing where I'd left off. It would return to the beginning so I'd have to try to find where I'd stopped. Extremely irritating. 4. I've gotten used to chatting with Alexa, although she doesn't always do as I ask. I can ask her to go to a particular movie and she says she will but then it turns out the movie isn't available. If you're so smart, Alexa, they why don't you know which movies are available? So there's that . . . .

title: Eats Data like crazy, remote size difficult to grasp.
body: First off this device will eat all of your internet GB. I ended up with over $20 in overages and only used it less than 2 weeks. I only bought it because Amazon prime was no longer going to be compatible with my Sony streaming device. (and in all the years I have had my Sony I never once went over my allowance of 150 GB). Plus Amazon offered this with a $25 off coupon. I really wish I would have purchased the Roku instead (everyone tried to tell me to get Roku). Next the remote is very tiny and difficult to grip. I have small hands so I can only imagine how hard it would be for larger hands. And although it only has a few buttons they are located in such a way that its not easy to reach with your thumb. I do like the voice feature.....because this device/remote makes you use too many steps to get you back and forth between screens. Finally it took forever to get the device. I was to have express shipping and I had to reorder it. Even with the replacement order took almost a week to arrive and I was to have next day delivery. I have since changed the streaming quality to a lower setting and set limits for not going over. Since I haven't had it a full month yet I'm not sure what will happen this internet bill cycle. If it eats up data like last cycle (less than 2weeks) I will be discontinuing using it.

title: Great concept BUT
body: Like many, Shelter in place has caused my family to change how we do things compared to before. Let me start by saying I am a huge fan of ROKU and have been using them for years ,, but sadly ROKU does not offer an Internet browser option. We wanted to be able to stream our Churches Sunday services and at the time when I purchased te Fire TV, a browser streaming was the only option. So based on reviews and that Fiery TV does have a browser, I purchased one. The Setup is very simple and it does offer a nice selection of apps. The concept is great, however as many have experience, the issue with Buffering is ridiculous. and then when it does start the buffering, it resets my screen resolution to a lower and poor quality resolution.I have tested Internet and have a very solid connection 30mbs plus through the Fire TV, and just incase there might have been the slightest chance of a weak signal, I connected a dedicated Router within 5 ft of the Fire TV. Have turned off any and all un necessary apps and services and still BUFFERING. I can mirror my phone using the same internet connection and never see any buffering.I so regret that I purchased this and because I wanted to see this work and spent so much time trying to get it to work, I am past my return period.

title: Critical Feature is Missing
body: This is my fifth Firestick. Like all the others it is packed with features, provides access to Amazon Prime content and to Hulu, Youtube, and many others that I do not use. It also allows side-loading of Kodi and VLC media players that I use to access my collection of videos on my home cloud server. So what is missing? When I installed it on an older 1080p high def TV I noticed that the picture was cropped; the magnifying glass to the left of the home menu was almost entirely cut off. When I went to the display option in the settings menu I found that the scaling tool available in earlier Firestick models had been disabled. In its place was a suggestion that I should use the TV's scaling feature instead. Well, my old TV had grayed out the controls that I would need to fix the problem. I checked my other four Firesticks, and found that three of them have a functional scaling feature, while the fourth one is like this newest model (scaling disabled). I spent an evening rotating the various Firesticks to different TVs, and I found that my newest smart TVs are able to automatically scale the Firestick picture, while older TV's need the scaling feature built into the older Firesticks. It seems the Firestick picture needs 4% scaling. Shame on Amazon for disabling this critical feature.

title: Lifespan is too short, fails way too quickly
body: I've purchased 4 of these in the past few years, and was excited to get the Recast DVR which requires Firesticks to function. But 2 of the 4 have already failed, both just beyond the one year point. I was expecting something more like the Roku's that I've owned, which never seem to fail and tend to outlive their usefulness as they can't keep up with all the app updates thru the years. But Firesticks must be made with cheaper components to keep the cost down. So if you're cord cutting, you'll probably need to factor in a new Firestick every 18 months or so.Googling for help I found many others having the same frustration and error. The stick connects to the router, but keeps losing the actual internet connection, even though connected. It starts intermittently, but soon refuses to connect and you're stuck.Tech support was very pleasant and tried their best, but after the usual, reboots, factory resets etc. they gave up as well.It's not a bad device, but I hope that Roku can also stay in business after being undercut by Firesticks, and that they don't have to lower their quality as well to compete. $40 every 18 months isn't the end of the world, but it's a pain to mess with and I'd rather pay a little more for something I don't have to fuss with.

title: A triumph of form over function
body: Clearly whomever designed the Firestick was far more concerned with appearance than with ease-of-use. It is small and sleek... and utterly un-intuitive. I have a Master's degree and worked for over 20 years in IT. I am not intimidated by technology, and usually can figure out how gadgets work without having to turn to a manual. My employer recently got Firesticks for all the TV at our community. After messing about with it, I finally had t ask my boss how to use it. The damned thing doesn't have a number pad on it, so you have to navigate through a menu to select a range of numbers (0-4 or 5 - 9) and then you can pick the digit you need: woe to he who sets his amazon passcode to 19271: this would require selecting 0-4, then selecting 1, then selecting the 5-9 range, then selecting the 9, then again selecting selecting the 0-4 range, then selecting the 2.... and so on. PUT A DAMNED 0-9 KEYPAD ON THE DEVICE!!! Yes, it won't be all pretty and sleek, but it won't be pretty after I hurl the thing out of the window in frustration!And don't get me started on the ring that is used to select up, down, left and right.....I am SO glad I got to try this out at work before I was temped to by one at home. I am told the Roku is much more user-friendly.

title: Do you love yelling "what?  what? can you hear me?" into your phone, this product is for you
body: This product LOOKS like it'll be great...and when it works, IF it works, it is. You will be required to buy multiple remotes, which cost as much as the original device, and spend hours and hours with customer service. I don't know if you've used Amazon's call center before, but it goes like this. You call the number and get full volume static and lots of cross talk with a tinny voice in the distance saying "hello? Hello?". This has happened on every phone, from every location I have ever tried. When you give up and hang up, they will call you back, on a connection so crystal clear you can hear a pin drop. Don't get your hopes up. In a second or two, you'll be rerouted to India, via Neptune, on Amazon's state of the art communication system, comprised of tin cans, wire and old CB radios.You'll have lots of time to enjoy the static, and the shouting "can you hear me" back and forth, because no decisions of any kind can be made by the person you are shouting with... all questions and decisions require lengthy holds while your shouting companion "checks". If you are hoping to enjoy trouble free TV, you might want to consider a different purchase, or stock up on remotes so you can replace them every few months and skip the customer "service".

title: Worked Once - Never Again
body: Plugging the Firestick into a Samsung smart TV, it worked well for a bit. There is (was) an issue with Xfinity when the Firestick asked for the ID and password for my Xfinity account. After entering it about fifteen agonizing times, it still refused to connect. Same thing with my Amazon Prime account, which is claimed to stream History and many other channels. Netflix worked.Now, my TV refuses to see the Firestick. I have upgraded the software on the TV, rebooted it many times, plugged and unpluged for several days to drain all the electronics. I have plugged and unplugged the Firestick, plugged it into various HDMI connections on the TV.The TV claims the Firestick is "no Anynet+ device connected." Yet, the TV does know which HDMI port the Firestick is plugged into.When the Firestick actually worked, it was clear and effective. Now, it is simply a useless blob of plastic, and I am a blob of blubbering insanity.I am willing to work on this, but can not get any menus or handles to work on. Beware.Update 20200123 - Firestick and Samsung smart TV will not, will not, will not talk. I have rebooted Firestick, rebooted TV, updated software in TV, updated Firestick.Can anybody help"?Thank you.

title: :-(  HORRIBLE  NIGHTMARE - NOTHING MORE THAN A MARKETING PLOY TO GET MORE & MORE $$$  :-(
body: *Poor!!! Uses HUGE amount of bandwidth:-(Cache fills up very quickly causing streaming & use to a slow crawl. You are forced to PAY more to BUY apps try to clean the cache. Takes alot of time patience & FRUSTRATION to set up... Let alone all the time you have to waste on trying to do constant mainainence .** WORST of all, when FireStick was physically removed from Sony Bravia TV all the Sony apps previously on the TV completely WIPED OUT & DELETED. We have wasted over 50 Hours trying to get the Factory Sony Smart TV & Apps some how loaded & reinstalled back on to our very expensive TV.* Do NOT use FireStick if connecting to your cell phone's data plan or a Hotspot (mifi) - even if you have an "UNLIMITED" plan - they all cap & throttle the data you are able to actually use also they will say you are ROAMING when in actuality, you are still on their local network - which costs you even more & slows your data connection - often slower than 50k (even slower than Dial-Up of over 20+ years ago)>> Don't even bother with buying a streaming device such as Firestick unless you have a DSL or Fiber connection!!!+ The firestick did have ALEXA - a fairly decent voice recognition when it actually does actually work.:-(

title: A good product, but does not last
body: First, the steam quality is very good as long as your WiFi is fairly close. Plaster walls really can hurt the connection though - keep that in mind if you live in an older house. On another bright note, the user interface is very simple and effective.However, this Fire Stick, which was purchased on November 19, 2019, lasted about a year and a half (until March 17, 2021). If we go ahead and purchase another Stick, it will be our fourth in five years. Perhaps my expectations are too low, but that's a ridiculous replacement rate for a product that I would expect to last at least 2.5-3 years. Almost all of our Fire Sticks have failed just beyond the guaranteed replacement window (funny how that happens, right?). All two previous failures and now this failure have all stemmed from the Fire Stick failing to connect to our WiFi - I suspect the Stick's network card fails.I attributed the first two failures to just receiving poor product, and gave Amazon the benefit of the doubt. However, three strikes is enough for me - I will not buy another Fire Stick.TLDR:It's a good product, but just be aware that you will likely need to replace every 1-1.5 years. If you're willing to do that, then this is the product for you!

title: Love the function. Hate the bugs.
body: Updated: This generation of the Fire Stick routinely drops the Wi Fi settings, is unable to connect, and/or dumps the password. It frequently requires a power recycle to wake it up. I do not recommend this product.——————- I like the function of the Fire Stick; this is my second one on a second TV. The Fire Stick is a huge improvement to the “Smart TV” logic and is easy to use, when it works. My first Fire Stick is an older version that has been rock solid reliable. So, with the confidence of owning an older version I added a second Fire Stick to my house. This new Fire Stick occasionally does not wake up when power is applied to the TV, has lost network connection (the router is within 3 feet of the Fire Stick), and apps sometimes lock up. Issues got so bad that I returned the product to try another, with similar results. So, Amazon has some work to do, namely to provide a software update that allows the user to turn off Sleep mode, and smooth the integration of app execution.I like the added function of the all-in-one remote (controls the TV power and volume on the Fire Stick remote), which is an upgrade from the 1st Gen Stick. But, for whatever reason, Amazon dropped the ball on the details with this revision.

title: Good when it works but annoying when it doesn’t!
body: The fire stick is very cool and has all the apps I wanted including YouTube TV but for some reason it just randomly stops working when I am watching stuff and it’s so frustrating. Two stars because I expect it to work all the time and it’s fairly new only about a month or two old. The first few times I just thought it was a fluke technical issue but now I think I got a bad stick or it is poorly made because I can’t use it anymore since the last time it froze. I let it sit for a day nothing. Two days still nothing. I don’t know why it’s not working but I’ve seen others complain of this issue. I need a refund or exchange because I did not buy this with the intention to use it every once in a while when it feels like working. I want to be able to use it every day for as long as I want without it freezing and going out on me. On top of all that the voice recognition doesn’t work. Every time I try to talk to it, it doesn’t pick anything up. I was debating between this roku and Apple TV and I feel I made a mistake since it doesn’t work. It freezes and stops working for the rest of the day and now for multiple days. Not ok!Also it says to connect to wall for better quality but without connecting to outlet it doesn’t work at all

title: Easy setup, difficult maneuver
body: We decided to cut the cable and went to Playstation Vue with Firestick. The setup of the firestick and the Vue d/l were easy. The problems come in when trying to maneuver around Vue. The Firestick remote is severely limiting and confusing. Fast forward and rewind buttons don't work for fast forward and rewind, you need to use the left and right of the large round button. And you can only rewind in 10 second increments up to 53 seconds. So if you want to rewind to the beginning of something, it's painful! There are times when we want to control something, and Vue will say we need to hit a certain button, but that button doesn't exist on the firestick remote. Also, if you need to change channels, it will take while. You have to scroll through the entire guide. I tried the voice part of the remote. While the voice response says it is changing to that specific channel (NBC, Fox sports, etc.) it doesn't change the channel. I've read this is a problem with the Firestick on some streaming services. I invested in three of these for our TV"s, and disappointed. I would look elsewhere for your streaming controls. Simplicity is sometimes good, but not in the case of the firestick remote. It's actually painful.

title: Buy a Roku streaming device instead!
body: I bought two of these at about the same time for two different TVs in my house. I had problems with both of them almost from the beginning. Set up is easy, and if they actually work for you, the quality is good. The content is adequate. The problem I have had with both of the ones I bought is that they constantly freeze up. They also reboot in the middle of programs on a regular basis. Most of the time, I can't get the devices to wake up without unplugging and plugging them back into power. With one of them, I called for support and they wanted to sell me another remote at a "discount" of $10 less than buying a new stick and remote. I passed and trashed that device entirely. The one I have left is getting worse. My Roku streaming stick, that I've have longer than both of my Fire TV sticks has never giving me one problem ever. It just works and works and works. I've even taken it other places and put it on other TVs, and it never gives me problems. I'm a tech guy for a living and so I know how to troubleshoot issues, and I've done everything from cable swaps and power supply swaps (always with another of the same) to various ports on various TV's. The Fire TV sticks never got better. Seriously, buy a Roku.

title: So disappointing
body: I absolutely hate that these are so terrible. I love the platform and Alexa capabilities, but what is the point in having something that constantly freezes? I have called twice within a week and the first rep logged my issue as something entirely different than what I told her. So, now that I'm on the phone with the second he is saying this is a different issue! No! The firestick says the homescreen is unavailable which causes you not to be able to use any applications. I check my network status and it shows very good connection. After a few mins home comes back up, I play my show and it freezes in the middle. I have 2 firesticks that this is happening on. The rep I'm currently on the phone with keeps telling me it's my internet connection and putting me on mute when I try to explain the situation. My warranty expired 2 days ago but he is attempting to work around that. Supposed to receive a call in 2 days and will update then as to what happens next.Update: I ended up getting a "exchange" for only one of my messed up sticks. It arrived and was refurbished. Still having trouble with the firesticks freezing and not coming on at all sometimes. Very disappointed in Amazon.

title: Two Firestick have failed after warranty expired
body: I just purchased my 3rd Firestick. Well the first one I received as a gift in 2017 but found it too hard to use at the time and I already had a Smart TV that gave me the streaming sources I was interested in. Last year, I decided to cancel my DirecTV subscription and switch to streaming with YouTubeTV. I couldn't download the YouTubeTV app to my TVs. I reinstalled the Firestick I was gifted in 2017. I ordered another Firestick for my other TV. The gifted one stopped working after about 6 months so I purchased another one. Then, the 2nd one that I had purchased stopped working after 90 days. That was very disappointing.When I contacted Amazon about it via chat, their representative said they would credit me $99 for the two failing Firesticks even though the 90 day warranty expired. I never received the refund and got in touch with them again. This time I was told I didn't get the $99 because the warranty expired. Instead, they offered me two 15% discounts. I used that today to buy a new Firestick. This time I added the 2-year protection plan for $4.99. I hope the Firestick that I have and the new one that I just ordered keep working for 2 years and more.

title: Great content, dodgy functionality - edit 2 stars now
body: Since I wrote the original review I've tried multiple times to reinstall Netflix and it won't work. I've rebooted the Firestick and deleted and added the Nexflix app over and over. It just spins on Installing. Other apps installed no problem. So the Stick is a piece of garbage if I can't watch Netflix. Original review follows.As in the headline the content is great. I cut the cord a couple of years ago largely because I could get most of what I want to watch through the Fire Stick (Prime, Netflix, Hulu, etc.)The hardware and software on the other hand are unreliable enough to be frequently annoying and occasionally enraging. Right now for no apparent reason, I've had to re-register my device and am in the process of reinstalling the apps. In the registration process it asks what apps you have but the ones I selected aren't appearing on the home screen. So I'm having to search for them and activate one at a time.On the merely annoying front, the device loses connectivity daily and I have to unplug, replug to reboot it. And the original remote died after about seven months. Overall I'm happy but Amazon could do better.

title: No Kindle book reader. Low memory, not room for many apps. Great for Amazon Prime Video!
body: Pros:* Apps run well. It’s a pleasure to use this for Amazon Prime Video! Easy to see what is available and to navigate the app. Being able to do X-ray while the movie is playing is pretty cool.* The built-in IMDB app is pretty good. Lots of good shows there. Good thing since it can’t be uninstalled.* Internet browser is ok. Have to use the remote so it’s a little clunky, but it works. But can’t use it to load up the Kindle web reader app! I tried! :(* I have an Echo, a couple of Dots and also Alexa-connected Phillips Hue house lighting. The Alexa integration on the the remote is great. I use it all the time for the lights. Also, when you use the remote to ask for the weather, it briefly flashes on the TV. Thanks to this, I was able to turn off the mic on the TV Room Dot. So that means less chance TV sound will accidentally wake up Alexa...Yay!* Fire TV initial setup asked me what brand TV I have, and set up the correct power, volume and mute controls on the Fire TV remote. Nice touch.Cons*No Kindle App. You give me Freetime & Gamecircle that I’ll never use and yet there’s no Kindle app. The Kindle Keyboard device is the thing that first got me to Amazon, back when all they did was books. That’s their original thing and yet you don’t give me a Kindle app on the Fire TV? What a missed opportunity, Amazon! I’m trying to stay away from small screens as much as possible to keep eye strain to a minimum, and the Fire Stick obviously works fine for reading WaPo, so where is the thinking on no Kindle app? Amazon wake up. Give us our Kindle books. Lol.*Low Memory. I would love to try The Great Courses Plus or even HBO, or Acorn but I don’t think I could run more than one of them. By the time I installed Netflix, YouTube, WaPo, the Internet Browser and MSNBC, and a couple of small utilities, I didn’t have a lot of memory left. I know Android requires operating memory. Missed opportunity, but thanks for making room for Gamecircle and Freetime. ;)*WaPo app is limited to a few free stories. It will not let me log in to my account so that I can have the full news like the full app on my phone will give me. Doesn’t the Fire Stick run on Android? Wouldn’t it be worth the time to convert the Android version over? People like me that have to wear readers would love to be able to take them off and read the news across the room. That’s another missed opportunity for Amazon!Additional thoughts:I guess I was spoiled by having a Roku. It’s an old one, I bought it in 2012 but I never had storage problems. Even now that it’s 8 years old, I have 22 apps on it and no memory problems. Really the only reason I bought this Fire Stick is because Amazon Prime Video and the YouTube app both run so slow on my old Roku. They have become bigger and slower as more features were added and sometimes they actually cause the Roku to restart. The newer Rokus have a faster processor, so I was going to get a new Roku, but then I saw this on sale with a promo code for half price. I know how Android requires operating memory, but I noticed that this HD version had the same 8 gb memory as the 4K one, so I figured if that’s what they would use in the higher priced one, I’d be ok. I didn’t count on so many Amazon apps bloating the device. I never intend to play games, I don’t have kids, I use Google photos for pictures, etc etc. Why can’t I delete these unused apps? It’s not like Amazon is receiving any financial gain from them mandatorily taking up space? If I had more room I’d be signing up for HBO and The Great Courses Plus at the very least...but again, Amazon shoots themselves in the foot by not giving us enough memory. I’d even pay a little extra for more memory but you don’t give that option.So, I’m keeping my Roku hooked up and I’ll use it for the apps it does ok on...Maybe by conserving operating memory on the stick, I could try out HBO, PBS or The Great Courses, and hopefully not have memory issues like some have reported. I don’t imagine I could run all three of them though. :(I just don’t understand why there aren’t more reviews talking about this. All those people using HBO and other channels along with Netflix, Hulu, etc. why aren’t they talking about having memory problems? I know this will be lost in a sea of reviews but I hope someone does read it and finds it helpful! :)

title: Roku Stick vs Fire Stick and YOUTUBE TV!
body: My family recently “cut the cord” and decided to go with youTube TV (YTTV) for our TV needs. We had older streaming devices that weren’t compatable with YTTV so we upgraded to new ones, one of which was this Fire Stick. Since I also own the Roku Stick (and Roku Stick+), I’ll provide a comparison.Starting/setting up for the first time:The Fire Stick took forever, compared to the Roku Stick. Part of what took so long was the updating of the software. The device also restarted a couple of times, once after the software update and once after the remote pairing. It also abruptly restarted after the initial selection of the software language and network selection. Roku’s initial set up was much quicker and more fluid in comparison. I’d say the Fire Stick took 4 times as long to setup.Remote:Both the Roku Stick (updated version, not the purple stick) and the Fire Stick have “smart” remotes with the ability to interpret commands using the remote mic. Roku’s remote is less “smart” in that it is unable to perform commands like playing a certain show on a certain app. The Fire Stick outperforms in this area. I also like the feel of the Fire Stick’s remote in hand. It has a slimmer profile and the buttons are smoother and more intuitive, in my opinion. I can easily use the remote without glancing at the buttons. I also appreciate the ability to pair the Fire Stick remote with a home theater device. Roku’s remote can only pair to a TV. Luckily, my LG TV with the Roku Stick offers the ability to control the soundbar through the TV, so I lucked out. Again, the Fire Stick is better in this area. However... and this is a biggie—WHY on Earth is the remote compartment so dang hard to open?! I had to reference the illustrated instructions more than once to make sure I wasn’t missing something. It was incredibly frustrating to not be able to do something as simple as install batteries into the remote. After trying for a good five minutes (or more), I was finally able to slide the battery compartment cover off, with the assist of a flathead screwdriver. I sure hope the batteries last a while because I’m certainly not looking forward to removing the cover again.App interfaces/User experience:You know, I’ve always been a Roku user so I’ve been used to their menu and search options. The Fire Stick menus feel vastly different. Navigating isn’t difficult, but I enjoy the Roku experience better, in terms of exploring new apps, movies and shows. However, this Fire Stick is going in my son’s room so I’m loving the easy access to Prime Video shows, which are primarily what he watches. Another big plus for me is the parental control options on the Fire Stick. My son is too young to surf on his own, but I like the ability for further down the road.Picture quality and overall performance:Picture quality is on par with the Roku Stick. What surprised me was how often this device buffered and lagged. I’m not sure what was going on, but on the first attempt to stream straight out of the box, the device lost network connection several times. The internet signal was fine, but the device’s network connection was unstable. I called down to my husband who was watching a show on the Roku and asked if he was having issues. Nope, he wasn’t, so the issue was isolated to the Fire Stick. I’ve since used the Fire Stick again and haven’t had the same issues so I’m hoping it was just a fluke. Still, this is no way to start off with a new device.In all, I really like the Fire Stick, but when compared to the Roku Stick’s performance, I’ve got to give it to Roku. A product is only as good as it is reliable, and as of right now, the Roku product is appearing to be more reliable. However, I think the Fire Stick has some very great qualities and features. It is larger in size when compared to the Roku Stick and Roku Stick+, which makes it less desirable with the projector I’m using it on, but I appreciate the included extender cable.Oh, and lastly, but certainly an important thing to note: YOUTUBE TV IS NOW SUPPORTED ON THE FIRE STICK AS OF TODAY! I almost returned the Fire Stick because I wasn’t able to stream YTTV, but I held off hope that it would be offered in the future. Well, I received the email this morning that YTTV is now supported on this device and I promptly downloaded the app and have been able to stream it perfectly. Yay!

title: Small package, big uses **updated 9/19/21**
body: First this is a 3 star review but I really like it. It's only that way because to me the cons are things that are annoying and prevent me from enjoying it fully the way I want. Saying that, this doesn't mean that others won't enjoy it the same or more just because of me being nitpicky.Pros1. Small device. Most devices like this are boxes that take up room. So something that is the size of a large thumb drive and is hidden is great.2. Small minimal remote. The remote itself is barely bigger than the device which is nice. It also has minimal controls.3. Available apps. There is hundreds if not more apps availble for the device. It takes a little bit of research and digging but you can pretty much find anything you want to keep you entertained.4. Simple and very easy to setup and use. Not tangled cords or box to collect dust.Cons1. Storage space was already half full. It is a little ridiculous that the devices comes with 8 gigs of storage space and nearly half of it is already used before you even register the device and install apps. After a few apps that increases to over half. Doesn't really make sense. But Amazon is known for this. I also have their Kindle Fire tablet and it's the same thing there. I wish they'd let you uninstall and keep uninstalled apps you don't intend to use. I don't want to use Amazon music, photos or most other Amazon apps.2. Performance is a little slow. Again I get it, it's cheap(or at least when I bought it the price was $24.99) but it's a little slow on processor speed. Wifi works great so I know it's not that. More ram would help with load times of apps. Better optimized OS would as well.3. The fact you can't use one Amazon account to register the device to and use another to watch Prime. I used my account to register then used my sisters account with her knowledge and permission for Amazon Prime. I couldn't because I had to use the account registered to the device to run Prime. Doesn't make sense honestly.4. No Micro SD slot. That would have been great to have to open up space. Being able to put video and audio on it to play off of. Or install apps onto it so you don't use all the space on your device.OverallIt's a great device and one that has loads of promise and uses. It just needs some tweaking to make it perfect which it is close to being.I'll update this review every so often as it's needed.**updated 9/19/21**My review stays the same for the stars given and pros. However cons increased because honestly the firestick just can't handle new apps.First about 8 months we started having issues with Hulu and it jumping around and sometimes freezing or kicking us out to the home screen. It only did this with Hulu and not Netflix. I think it's ad related and I don't think the firestick can handle them and running apps. This happened on both firesticks. It wasn't heat related as these issues would happen as soon as we started using Hulu and the firestick for the first time of the day. The other issue came recently and with Paramount +. Same issues as Hulu except worse. Granted this could be a limited issue to just our firesticks but I don't think it is. I think with the limited space and operation specs the firesticks can't run apps that have ads very well.

title: They say it works with your Echo, it does and doesn't
body: I wanted to control my Cable Box with the Alexa Remote. It seems like it should do it. And after 3 hours with Tech Support we agree that it could do it. But it doesn't.I have streaming on my Xbox, my Blu-Ray and my Smart TV. I don't need the streaming. Thought the Fire Stick would be great for doing movies in the backyard and for my Mother-in-law's house. And the remote would allow her to turn on the TV with her voice, using either the remote or the Echo Dot she has. I bought two. One for her and one for me.3 Hours later, I know now the product that could do that is the Cube--I think. The Stick, though marketed as if it does, does not. The remote could, but the software prevents it from doing so.Even though the setup of the Fire Stick lets you setup your cable box, and during the setup cues the cable box with IR to advance the channels. And even though the Fire Stick can pull up the IR codes for your Alexa Remote to use to work your Cable Box...it won't do it. EVEN THOUGH, after the set-up, the screen tells you it can...just say, "Tune to CBS"...it can't.Frustrating, right? It has the IR capabilities to send a signal to the TV to change to channel to 5-oh-whatever. It knows CBS is on channel 5-Oh-Whatever. It knows my cable provider and can pull up the channel info and the IR codes for my Cable Box. It has a Microphone. The Fire Stick is linked to my "Alexa Network"...but the software will neither allow the remote to trigger the IR to change the channel nor will it send my command across the "Alexa Network" and across the internet to digitally change the channel on the cable box (like my Echo Dot does.)The cable box can be controlled by my Echo Dot (thank you Frontier) via the App, which means it is controlled by my voice by sending a command from my Dot digitally across the vast internet to my cable box...this works.If, like me, you thought the remote, based on the advertisement, linked into this network and worked like another DOT, it does not. The Alexa Remote does NOT network with your Echo Dot and will not send a command from it's microphone to the Dot across the "linked devices". Therefore the Dot will not change your cable box. It could. It's just some simple lines of code. But it won't. The Dot will link to the Fire Stick, the Alexa Remote will link to the Fire Stick, but the Remote and the Dot won't link up thru the Fire Stick.Many may never have this problem, it did seem like I was the first person to bring this question to Tech Support, but if you were like me and thinking this would basically make your TV another Echo Dot, it will and it won't. It did link up and allow me to control my lights and the light group. But the cable box...the one thing I wanted that little remote to control, so that my wife and mother-in-law could say, "Alexa, turn on the TV to CBS" and the TV comes on and switches to the right input and the cable box comes on and switches to the right channel and the soundbar comes on and switches to the proper input...that remote will do 2 out of 3 of these things.For that reason, I sent them both back.

title: Works pretty well, though the UI could use an overhaul for true usability
body: If you really like worming around the screen with a remote, the UI might be half-usable.If you want to use a keyboard and mouse, only a few tiny portions of it will even respond to anything beyond, at best, the arrow keys and maybe the page up/down if you're lucky. You get a circular mouse cursor whenever moving it, but there's no response to left or right clicks for virtually anything either in the main Firestick UI, or the apps included on it (or in anything I've installed so far, but not counting those against the Firestick).Some things will actually let you type on the keyboard (some passwords, logins, some search stuff), most won't take input from it. Or, you can type but it doesn't respond to the input correctly, sometimes acting as if you had hit the return key while you're still entering characters.The "HDMI extension cable" that's included is just about useless, being only a couple of inches long. If it were just two feet long, it would actually be useful, letting you put the Firestick up above or below the monitor, where it would clear whatever metal/etc is inside it to let it get a better signal. Instead, you have to go buy a *real* extension cable, and throw away this little wannabe. Waste of materials, manufacturing capability, and shipping costs.A longer USB cable would also be helpful, perhaps twice as long as the included one, to be able to reach the outlets from where the TV itself is. Instead, you also have to buy a longer cable here, too. Thankfully, this one is long enough to be used as a regular USB charging cable for other devices, so it isn't a complete waste.One really frustrating issue with setup is that you cannot use a BT keyboard to enter any of your information for setup, especially things like long wifi passwords and the like, because you can't pair anything with the Firestick until *after* you have completed setup. I don't know if you can use a regular USB keyboard to do it, as I didn't have a USB hub or splitter handy to try on it.Most of the rest of it is pretty good, and it works well enough even with only a "fair" wifi signal, better than I expected it to.I wish it were possible to uninstall or delete preinstalled apps, because most of them I don't want anything to do with, and would prefer to get them off my screen out of the way.I also wish it had the ability, like the web-browser versions of the various sites do, to "thumbs up" or "thumbs down" various shows, so that it stops trying to suggest things I want nothing to do with and don't want in my way when looking for other shows.I also wish it would remember what I have already watched on the web-browser versions on my PC, but it doesn't have any idea--it does know I watched the shows, or at least that they are on my watchlist, etc., but ti does not know which episodes/etc that I have watched, or where I was within one that I was in the middle of watching.

title: Study and compare!!! Or atleast read this!
body: Awesome firestick!Give 5 stars if your elderly, technologically illiterate, or want something simple.Give only 1 or 2 stars if you know what you are doing, have cable TV, hulu TV, youtube TV, Philo Tv, and want to be able to add dozens or hundreds of apps from your favorite TV or premium channel movie providers.Though firestick is awesome, I should have went with the $45, 15gb one, this one is only 4gb, and you will be continually deleting apps you don't think you use just to make room ones you want. Even when you didn't really want to get rid of other apps that you use some. Which dousnt even include mentioning gaming apps like bowling, pinball, gambling games such as 21, um, blackjack, poker, etc.If you have lots of interest and are about adding as much as you can, do yourself a favor and get the more expensive 15 gb firestick.Wish I would have known. Too late now. Dont make the same mistake.Great if you have hulu, Netflix, Amazon prime, Philo, YouTube tv, and that is all you want along with maybe some great free great apps like Tubitv, etc.But if you have all that and youtube tv, cable TV, etc, then go with the more expensive firestick, then you can ad Disney, discovery, history, and much more of your favorite basic channel and premium channel apps for free just because you have cable. Because many of your favorite channel apps have hundreds of movies and TV series with VOD (video on demand). Plus like I said, you'll be able to add dozens or hundreds of games.Stuff I wish I knew, or I wouldn't have bought this one.Know what you are doing and want tons of stuff, get more expensive, this one holds almost crap. If you want hundreds of channels and games, expecially if you are a cable subscriber er, which includes youtube TV or Hulu TV, because many favorite channel apps full of VOD Content now include hulu TV, youtube TV, as you having a paid cable subscription. This entitles you to all VOD (video on demand content) basicly for free, without having to pay more. Free as part of you paying for cable.Like I said, I wish I knew all this before getting this cheaper firestick that dont hold as much. Is ok if you only want 30 apps or so like hulu, Netflix, YouTube tv, pluto, Amazon prime,But if you want CNN, CBS, Disney, discovery, history, animal planet apps, etc and can get all there VOD content for basicly free because you have paid cable subscription in real world or online, then get more expensive firestick, then have all that plus enough room to download firestick games, poker, etc.

title: 90 day warranty; customer service no help; audio distortion
body: Update 2/15/20: while the Fire TV stick is still quirky and doesn't quite communicate with Alexa (shouldn't I be able to turn FireTv off via Alexa? or at least by using the power button on the remote???), it does seem as though the sound distortion was caused by the HDMI port of the BENQ monitor. I used a Samsung monitor and my Echo device and the sound distortion has mostly gone away -- now I only get a sporadic crackle now and then. Still unhappy with the way things played out with customer service, however.Original review:When I purchased the fire TV stick to use with my BenQ monitor and Echo Dot, it took a long time to get it set up and running, but it seemed fine if a little quirky. For instance, sometimes I couldn't use the "recent" buttons -- I would have to go in through "your apps & channels" to get to a recently viewed program to have it play. Fine, whatever. A little clunky, but after my experience with the Kindle Fire, about what I was expecting.But a few weeks ago, the sound was occasionally distorted through my Echo Dot. I'd turn it on and off, and sometimes the problem would disappear for a little while before it came back. Now, however, the distortion is always there. I connected a Bose wireless speaker via Bluetooth. Same distortion. I connected the Bose via headphone jack to the monitor. No problem, except that I can only control the volume by pushing the buttons on top of the speaker.After searching and finding that, lo and behold, I am not the only one to experience sound distortionI just spent almost two hours chatting with various customer service agents, restarting the fire tv stick, resetting the darn thing to factory settings, reinstalling, re-registering, re-connecting my echo dot and Bose speaker, and, guess what? Same problem. Distortion. When the customer service agent wanted me to then work with an Echo specialist, I said no, and could Amazon please send a replacement. I was then told that they could neither replace the device nor refund the purchase price because the warranty was up in December. 90 days! This device comes with a less-than-three-month warranty. Buyer beware.When I asked the agent to send my a transcript of the chat, he said he could not but there was an option to have the chat sent to me after the chat ended. I saw no "end" option so I asked him to end the chat. He did not respond. I finally closed the window after twenty more minutes went by. No option to have the chat sent to me, though I did take screen shots.

title: Fire Stick itself is 4 Stars.  The Fire TV Remote is ZERO Stars.
body: [[VIDEOID:baa07bcef95e10875445be96dff76d51]] Because of the disparaging feelings that I have between the Fire Stick itself and the Fire TV Remote Control, I have to rate the two with different ratings.I give the Fire Stick itself FOUR stars. It does everything that I expected, however I have problems trying to set up the Power Switch on the Remote to switch my Sony Bravia TV's video Input between my Cable Box, and the Fire Stick. The Fire Stick Setup Screen for controlling my TV, needs far more options than the limited number available to be able to switch the TV's input between the Cable Box and the Fire Stick. I don't have a way to tell the Fire Stick to not touch the TV's power switch, and to just select the input menu and go up or down a fixed number of clicks when switching to, or away from the Fire Stick input.As for the Fire Stick's Remote Control; what can I say. This part of the Fire Stick package gets ZERO stars. If Amazon had asked for bids on the worst possible design for a TV Remote, they couldn't have picked a better choice than the one they went with. This design is completely worthless in both form and function.First off it's nearly impossible to hold in your hand because of the lousy rounded bottom and slippery plastic used in the case's manufacture. I can't see how a disabled person with the loss of a hand or arm could possibly hold and use this remote.Secondly, the tiny buttons are located so close to each other that I'm constantly pressing the HOME button by accident when I simply want to PAUSE the Video. The buttons are too close to each other, and critical selections like HOME and RETURN should NOT be located so close to regularly used buttons like START/PAUSE and FORWARD and REVERSE. Also, If you lay the Remote flat on a table and try to press the buttons on the Left or Right side, the case rocks to the Left or Right because there is no support under the buttons due to the rounded bottom.Third, the poor design of the Remote makes it next to impossible to open the back to access the battery compartment. I feel like I'm going to break something fragile just trying to get the back off.Lastly, the only way to lift and carry the Remote is to try and grab it on the side edges where the rounded bottom meets the flat top. This means I have to squeeze hard on the Remote between my fingers just to insure that I have enough grip to lift it up, without it slipping out from between my fingers.

title: No user manual or list of what device can do.
body: I have only tried playing with the stick a few times so I'm still learning. There must be lots more capabilities. But nothing tells me what they are or how to utilize them.I can say once you transfer over to HDMI on your TV, (nowhere does it tell you to do this) it makes Netflix faster and closer to how I watch Netflix on my phone.First time I plugged it in it did not auto recognize the stick so I was dead in the water.The next day I unplugged & started again & it finally did what I would call a "plug & play" and start installing.At one point today it started giving me a list on screen of all the Alexa abilities but after about 10 it just stopped. ??The speed on Netflix is impressive! Still don't have the ability to do fast forward and rewind a few seconds like the app on my phone.No user manual which tells me how to save movies on the 8 GB drive. I don't have any idea! How are we supposed to find out? Nothing in writing or a pdf manual that can be downloaded that is printable.There is nothing to tell me how to take it with me and use on a TV elsewhere.I need to find an Amazon rep in USA who can teach me what it can do and how.So far my experience is somewhat positive at least as a use of a very fast remote.But why do no Alexa devices have user manuals? I know it keeps changing but the basic list of abilities would be so helpful.Please Amazon, why?Oh, additionally, after not being able to remove the back to install the batteries after spending, would you believe 15 mins on it? (really, this is insane!), I asked two strong men to help with no luck. What the heck? 🙁While I was in my bank, I mentioned it to a teller who said she had the same problem; she tried unsuccessfully as well.Then a young man who worked there who had a firestick tried about 5 mins and & tries aided with a tiny item to pry it open slightly, he was able to open it!He showed me but it still stuck. It has to be the worse designed battery back opener in the history of remotes! 😟😟😟After the young guy opened and closed it a few times it loosened up enough that with the aid of the same tiny pry tool, it finally opened for me. I sure hope I don't have to take it back to the bank or to a tech shop when it needs new batteries! 😢My gosh! Who the heck is the design engineer who signed off on this design? 🙄🙄🙄

title: I'm sooo frustrated. I need to buy even more things to run Disney+ and FireStick?!?! -- UPDATED
body: Okay, Disney+ didn't like my smartTV. So I bought FireStick and was told that was all I needed. Now Firestick has arrived and it requires 1) available electrical outlet...where the TV, DVD player, game device and air filter are already plugged it, 2) a really long cable to reach from the wall mounted TV to the electrical outlet, and 3) a device to convert to ethernet instead of wi-fi. No one has been able to confirm for me if I HAVE to use wi-fi. Because, my smartTV runs better ever since I cabled it (ran direct ethernet cable). And why can't FireStick work off the power of the TV and require it's own power? I'm feeling really unsafe about so many devices plugged into the same outlet...and my rental apartment has a paucity of outlets.UPDATED: So I stood on one leg and balanced like this just to be sure the FireStick worked before I ordered additional parts. (Note: we now can't see TV from couch; however, this test was a proof of function.) I did NOT need to buy an ethernet converter. The FireStick connected just fine without ANY menu or set up adjustments on my smartTV. HOWEVER, the instructions are wildly lacking!!! Seriously, add to the diagram of the remote ALL of the working features. (i.e., pressing "play" does not mean "enter".) Show an example of the screens we should see so that we know if we are on the right path! I spent a lot of time on hold for and then with customer service. They asked if I was on the "menu" screen. I said "yes" because it was a menu screen. However, there are a gazillion menu screens depending on what part of the app one has reached. How much has this all cost? In a crude estimate if my time is $100/hour...I paid $70 for annual subscription to Disney+ which necessitated buying FireStick ($30) which necessitated buying an outlet adapter/expander, plus a cable long enough for my wall mounted TV to reach the outlet ($40), plus 2 hours of time with customer service and on hold ($200 value of my time) even though I used the "call me" feature which left me on hold for 30 min. before speaking with someone, plus one hour of time to research the adapters and cables ($100 value of my time) (including the ethernet adapter that was recommended but I never actually needed). So, buying Disney+ cost me about $440. All this so that my kid can be more occupied and allow me to get back to work from home. Seriously?!?!

title: Works great until it doesn't!
body: I own 2 Fire sticks and 2 4k Fire sticks. Overall have loved these devices aside from the barrage of Amazon-pushed ads and inability to fully control what you want to see up front. However, my most heavily used non-4k stick recently got frozen in a loop of upgrading - stuck mid-way then rebooting and going thru this sequence unending. I found the extremely difficult to find "Chat" section in Amazon where you can chat with a customer service rep. We tried to trouble shoot with all the reset and factory reset settings - without resolution. Ultimately, the rep got back on the line and said my device is broken and cannot be fixed as it is out of warranty. I purchased in Nov 2018 and no Jun 2020 it is worthless! It automatically updates and during this process it died. He offered me a one-time 15% discount off of a single item sold and shipped by Amazon. Now I shop constantly on Amazon, using my Amazon Rewards Visa card as many do. My first thought to replace like for like - but then why would I buy a device which can apparently fail rather easily shortly after the warranty fails? So I looked at a competing Roku device - ultimately deciding to wait a few months as an updated version was due in the fall. I also need a new TV, so I researched and selected one (sold and shipped by Amazon) placed it in the cart and no discount was there.So I opened another chat session (a week later) and armed with transcript from previous chat asked why no discount. This rep stated the discount is only good towards another Fire Stick. Lengthy conversation, but ultimately he said the other rep was incorrect and this is all that can be offered.I am NOT buying another device which I personally experienced failure on despite lack of abuse. I also mentioned to this same rep that I will write this review as well as start to reconsider my loyalty to Amazon site and Visa card. There are other sites and cards out there. Bottom line is this is an AMAZON manufactured product with their name on it and their forced-down-your-throat ads and it seemingly has obsolescence built into it as well. I am not tossing my other 3 but am not confident of their lifetimes and definitely will not replace them with Fire sticks. So much for making the customer happy :( .

title: I love streaming but mehhhh....
body: Let me first preface by saying I love Amazon. I may order something once a month and have the Prime service. I stream content from Amazon Prime probably every other day of some sort or other and love their original content: "All or Nothing" (NFL documentary series), "The Boys", Jack Ryan, etc.... I also use my Alexa and I wake up to 80s rock every morning.I guess my problem is 2-fold. I came from a Roku thumb-drive and decided to switch over to a Fire-stick because I liked the GUI better that I saw on my tablet. But once I saw it on my TV - I noticed a big problem. The GUI is a mess.With the ROKU finding a new release movie to watch is a breeze. Just go to Google Play and it opens up to everything right there. So simple. Each app in its own little box. Very vanilla.Trying to find all the new releases on the interface on a Fire interface and you'll have a seizure. Everything is scattered all over the place. Eventually I just wound up going on my android phone and finding the new movie I wanted to see what was out, then used the voice control (which is nice) on the Fire to search for it. It's that bad.The Fire interface syncs all the memberships you have on one screen so it tells you what movies you are able to watch - which in theory is nice. But it takes up so much real estate on the screen it makes me go cross--eyed. So you have your individual apps (movie streaming servics) across the top of the screen strewn horizontally. Directly above THAT are the most recent apps you were in. Below those are the individual services each listing movies/items you can see highlighted that you can watch. The screen just scrolls vertically forever.With Roku - so much smoother. So much less eye candy. So much simpler.The biggest issue I had though? Streaming reliability. I would have problems where while watching a movie it would lock up and tell me there was a problem connecting with the network and try re-establishing connection. I never had that problem before with the Roku. I had the Fire installed for 2 weeks and this would happen a couple of times a night.With the Roku before and after the Fire? No issues whatsoever.I'm sorry Amazon - this is one time where you lost me.

title: Promising Future
body: When comparing streaming devices, you'll find that each has its unique features and the goal is to find the right one for you. The Fire TV Stick is slow with poor wifi connectivity. The interface is also a mess, but at least they have improved the remote to add volume buttons. It was for these reasons that I chose the Roku Ultra, which I believe is the best device for the intended purpose.If you want more out of your streaming device, however, a Roku may not be your preferred choice. Many like the Fire TV Stick because of its seamless integration with Amazon Prime Video and/or Alexa. I couldn't care less about either, but I do like that the Fire TV Stick works with the Ring Doorbell (another Amazon product), while the others do not. I also like that the Fire TV Stick is more of an open platform, meaning that it's more customizable than others, like the Roku, which is essentially an appliance made to do one thing, but do it well.For example, the Fire TV Stick has web browsers. If you own a wireless keyboard, you can easily surf the web on your TV (nice for looking up programming information). And if you don't own a keyboard, it has an onscreen keyboard that works with the remote, which is mostly all I use, anyhow. In comparison, the web browser for the Roku simply streams your mobile web browser screen to your TV, which is sort of pointless, imo, since every device can do that and they don't even need a web browser app to do it.But what I really like most about the Fire TV Stick is the outlook for the future. Amazon recently introduced a "Live TV" menu, which for now is mostly meant for the Fire TV Recast DVR. It also works with Pluto TV now, and it's easy to imagine Amazon adding other apps in the future, so users will have a customized electronic programming guide. This is a feature that many cord-cutters argue is outdated and unneeded, but that depends on how you watch TV. And for those who spend time watching live TV, like I do, a built-in EPG is a huge asset.In summary, I have high hopes for the future of the Fire TV Stick, but it's just not there, yet. The developers seem headed in the right direction, but first it needs work on making the menu more navigable.

title: Configures with your TV power and soundbar.  Great signal strength.  Gets slow over time.
body: EDIT 02/21/2020I really liked the Fire stick for a while until the last few months I've noticed unresponsiveness from the UI, glitchiness, and general slowness. I currently have it plugged into an AC outlet to ensure it gets a constant stream of power. There are instances where the UI becomes unresponsive to the point where I have to reboot the device. When I scroll through the main Amazon Video UI, I experience all sorts of glitchiness and slowness. Sometimes it doesn't allow me to navigate through the titles and sometimes it's previewing the wrong title that I have selected. Trying to use Alexa is unresponsive and slow. I suppose that there is not enough computing power in these little sticks. I will probably look into getting the Fire TV Cube or a Roku device since they will have better processors.I finally configured it with my TV and my Yamaha soundbar. I was very surprised that it can control my soundbar too since it is plugged into the TV via an optical cable. Many thanks to the software developers and engineers on this! This configuration is found in settings, which walks you through every step and allows you to select from a very long list of soundbar manufacturers.The power button on the remote turns on my TV and since I have my soundbar powered by an energy saving surge protector, it turns on the soundbar only when the TV turns on. Saves on electric bills!I enjoy the user interface that the developers have created. The only thing that I haven't really used much is Alexa voice control. It seems to work well, but I generally don't use it. Will have to test further. I don't like that I can't search in YouTube using voice recognition. I think that they should add this feature. It would save me the hassle of typing out words letter by letter. The signal strength is great - I don't have to point the remote directly at the TV to get a response.Overall an amazing product. Would highly recommend. Google Chromecast is unfortunately inferior to the Fire Stick at this point.

title: HBO apps no longer supported as of 7/31/20; Device works well tho
body: Bought Fire TV stick to serve as main platform for all streaming apps: Amazon Prime Video, Netflix, Hulu, Disney+, HBO.My biggest gripe is that HBO apps are no longer supported on the Fire stick as of July 31, 2020. Hopefully they fix this but I cannot give this device full recommendation until they get HBO back. It's kind of ridiculous to have a device that is supposed to serve as an all-in-one tv streaming device that does not support every major streaming service.However, it works well, It's responsive. The remote setup to control my TV was almost instantaneous, which was an excellent surprise.The landing page/home page is a little messy and repetitive (different options displayed all over the place to enter the same app, which can be helpful but is also confusing and overwhelming at times. An example is Amazon video and IMDB TV have a relationship. IMDB TV content can be played through the Amazon app but IMDB also has it's own app which may not share the same play/episode history as when you accessed the content through the Amazon app. So the apps are connected but not integrated well which leads to more clicking if you try to resume playing your content through the wrong app).Resuming an episode after long pause is also an issue- If you pause an episode for too long the Fire stick will go to the screensaver mode. Too long in screensaver mode and the Fire stick exits you from the content and the app you are viewing, taking you back to the Fire Stick home screen when you wake it up. You will then have to manually navigate back to your desired streaming app and the content you were watching.Have not used voice recognition.All in all it's a nice little package whose interface is much better than the Amazon video apps that you find on Playstation and XBox gaming consoles.I'd recommend to those who don't use HBO but for those who do you may want to look to another streaming device until Amazon and HBO can get their poop in a group.

title: Sometimes you get what you paid for.
body: I purchased this as a cheap replacement for the Roku Stick that finally died on our family. After a few months of use, here are my thoughts on the product and why if given a second chance I would go with another product.First, the interface while nice has a tendency to crash, losing the internet connection and requiring a complete reboot of the stick to get functionality back. This has even required me to disconnect power to it because it has become completely unresponsive.Second, the app for the FireTV stinks. It will connect fine until it decides it doesn't want to anymore. So you suddenly find yourself without a remote when the actual remote has been lost in the couch cushions or your children has carried it away. Rebooting the Fire Stick is usually the only fix for the problem.Third, there is so little storage space on the stick itself that installing all the streaming services we were interested in or subscribed to meant that we frequently are informed that updates cannot install. We are not talking a lot of stuff, and it appears about half the storage is taken by shovelware from Amazon that you probably are not interested in (and have no way of removing). Amazon Photos, Amazon Music, Freetime, IMDB.tv, and a few others take precious storage space away from you. Also half a gig of storage will be dominated by a screensaver program that will automatically eat that space up even when you clear the cache (no way to have a simple screensaver because you might miss those Amazon Ads).So compared to the Roku we had, Apple TV, or even the Comcast Flex box; the UI is clunky, resource intensive, and bloated compared to the competition. I wish they would have invested the extra $10 in price to make it able to reliable do anything but shill Amazon products that are clunky on a TV. The only good thing for me is that it isn't built into the TV and I can remove it once I can afford a replacement.

title: Not Quite There
body: The way in which my household uses A/V equipment is in a home theatre configuration, which is all-Sony except for the cable box. Amazon's email notifying me that they were no longer supporting Prime Video via either my TV or BD-player was a disappointment. Amazon Prime Video had bailed us out of TV's summer and holiday doldrums for several years.I looked to the Fire Stick to get me back my Prime Video option. I ordered the Ethernet adapter since the cabinet is hard-wired with Ethernet. I read (possibly early) reviews that mentioned overheating fire sticks. That sensitized me to look for the potential problem. I noticed no overheating at all. I've got the fire stick and ethernet adapter wrapped together with a rubber band. And it's laid out where I can keep an eye on it.The hardware clearly needs a couple of additional LEDs: (1) to indicate power Off/On state; (2) to indicate that the microphone is active and listening. Now, it's true that the present "push to talk" behavior is effectively the same. However, I think many will agree that the mic LED is not going to be seen if a finger is covering it up.The documentation shipped with the Fire Stick meets only minimal requirements.For sure, the present documentation is enough to get the hardware installed, but then how do I use it with or without Alexa? I used it without Alexa (via the Stick remote only) for two solid weeks with no trouble. My attempt to use Alexa was disheartening; I'm going back to the remote method.Here's a synopsis of my session with Alexa:1) Alexa training is a matter of reading then speaking a sequence of one-liners. The problem is that, by the time I spoke the first half of a line, I forgot the second half.2) Alexa couldn't find my watchlist. It was much easier with the remote.3) Alexa seems not to be coordinated with TV episodes among several devices.

title: No way to disable sleep mode way bigger issue than it should be
body: I am not someone who is running a Fire Stick that has been hacked or cracked or unlocked or whatever it is all the kids do these days to get a bunch of illegal free stuff. I needed a device to update from a very old Apple TV to something that runs the new version of the Hulu app in order to allow my wife to watch Hulu stuff in the bedroom that she can't view on the old app on the old Apple TV. I'm not trying to edit command line settings on this thing in order to get it to do what I want.We have a needy dog and two kids and a lot of chores/laundry/etc going on all day long and it's not uncommon for us to pause watching something for a half hour to go deal with a bunch of stuff and want to come back and press play in order to keep viewing. Unfortunately, this stupid device goes to sleep, exiting the current app and taking you back to the home screen, at an automatcially set time THAT CANNOT BE CHANGED OR DISABLED. I would send it back, but my wife has decided to deal with the situation, but it doesn't make it less aggravating. if left on pause for more than twenty minutes, the Fire stick will go to sleep, exiting whatever app we are currently viewing things on and going back to the home screen, which then makes you have to boot the app back up in order to just keep doing what you were doing. People have been complaining about this feature for years, yet Amazon has not implemented a fix. I'd love to k now why.A slightly less surprising issue is that the thing doesn't do a great job of picking up WiFi. My 6 year old Apple TV streams better without buffering than this brand new Fire TV stick does, and they are literally next to one another in the bedroom.

title: It’s a mixed bag, but overall disappointing
body: I started with one fire stick in my bedroom and the more I used it the more I liked it. In fact I liked it so much that I talked my wife into cutting the cord (satellite) after 15 years. I was tired of paying for hundreds of channels that I NEVER watched.We ordered two more fire sticks for the house and a fire tv recast with an indoor antenna. I started noticing reception and system problems and associated it with the antenna so I ordered an rooftop antenna.Unfortunately, the problems persisted. While using this setup as a primary system it becomes glitchy. It constantly pauses with the three pulsating dots while watching live TV or recorded content from the recast. There are times when the system is unresponsive. There are times when you want to watch a channel, but it won’t tune (three pulsating dots), but other channels are fine. Watching football can be very frustrating when the stream hangs and then jumps to live and you’ve missed and entire play.All in all, I wish I would have just stuck with Directv and used the fire stick for watching movies or shows before bed. By the time I bought access for the content I wanted I didn’t save that much (maybe $30 per month), but the frustrations are mounting and I’m leaning towards cutting my losses on investing in this (>$300) and going back to directv anyway. At least with Directv I’m only frustrated with occasional disputes leading to channel outages and storms. With this system my wife and I get frustrated daily even with amazing content.

title: Not Aging Well...
body: This is a big device in a small package. With a strong wifi connection, you can stream flawlessly and get all your services in one easy to use home page. My only issue comes with the occasional Netflix service issues but I attribute that more to my wifi signal and only on my second floor through several walls.I own three Fire Sticks.
