The first is used on an older 720p TV in a bedroom that isn't compatible with apps. The second is a primary TV that's only about five years old but refuses to download certain apps like Disney+. The Fire Stick solved both problems. We recently dropped the wallet draining "triple play package" from Comcast and now rely solely on a high speed 1G Verizon Internet connection. The Fire Stick has replaced our old programming and we don't miss the 800+ channels of which we watched maybe five of them. Granted you need streaming service provider contracts but it's still cheaper. The Fire Stick is an easy to use device bringing all the services together in an aesthetically pleasing and user friendly way. I recommend it and probably even if you have a newer TV. I doubt its app formatting is as good. There's no monthly payment for the Fire Stick itself so it's still worth it.Update: One of my Fire Sticks updated with new software while the others wont do the same. Even with the update, I find it's not even close to being as convenient as the Chromecast device I recently bought. Amazon needs to improve the software and upgrade the memory and remote features.

title: Covers all the streaming basics, but very slow performance.
body: For the price this is a pretty OK streaming stick. All of the major streaming services are supported like Netflix, Prime Video (of course), YouTube, Hulu, etc. The device is easy to set up and takes up no shelf space since it is a small dongle you plug into the back of your TV. The remote works fine, and the latest version with volume and power control is great if you don't use other streaming devices or cable boxes on your TV as you can control most main TV functions with the one remote.The two main negatives I have with the Fire Stick is that it is VERY SLOW. It can take MINUTES for the interface to load with all of your app/movie tiles just so you can launch Netflix. It gets worse as you install more apps which brings me to the second problem: Lack of on-board storage.Of the advertised 8GB of storage space, the Fire Stick already has around 5GB taken up by the operating system and Amazon apps you can't delete like Amazon Photos. Since every feature is an app that requires installation this becomes a problem if you like to use a lot of different streaming services. Beyond that, the RAM on this thing is tiny and it appears to use storage space to offset the limited RAM so if you have more apps installed the performance of the stick degrades significantly.16GB should be the minimum amount of storage these things come with and 32 wouldn't be a stretch. Amazon sells a 32GB SD card for $6 so including that much storage wouldn't hurt the price of these that much.

title: It is slightly better and cheaper than equivalent Roku
body: I have Sling TV and several separate premium channel streams. This is maybe a review more of the channels and streaming than just the device. I also own some recent Roku devices as well to compare.Crunchyroll ad version for example and a few others will crash/corrupt in the middle of 'ad packs' at the same place consistently. This ruins the channel/app for at least the day. Roku channels don't seem to do ad packs for apps and so are immune. Late night streams weirdly crash more often for fire sticks as well. Sling TV's app quality isn't great and crashes at least once a day, but even the HBO app crashes once in a while. The Roku versions are a little more crashy and often triggers a watchdog full reboot while the Fire will come back to its menu instantly.The implementation of the apps UI is better than Roku. However, it seems that Amazon really wants users to search by Alexa instead of by text which makes a poor user experience when guessing titles.The hardware design is weird as it has to be very near the HDMI connection which makes supplying power harder. The device appears mostly responsive to the remote, much more than the Roku (both will be 'busy' and ignore remote commands once in a while). Overall it is good value as a streaming device and a better deal than the Roku equivalent. I hope Roku improves their hardware at similar price and capability points. Both need to improve codec error handling and remote responsiveness.

title: AMAZON CUSTOMER SUPPORT
body: So I was to get TWO products the firestick and another product I got my firestick and I like it no issues yet like I said I just got it but I have a complaint for AMAZON I HAVEN'T GOT MY SECOND ORDER SO I CALLED AMAZON GOT A gentleman I told him my issues stated that I was supposed to get my product today the same time I had got my fire stick he said okay let me see what's going on he says I do show IT in the system we don't know why you did not get your second item let me place you on hold, so I was on hold with Amazon 1 hour and 20 minutes no check-in not tell me what was going on just had me on hold I would get 30 seconds of music and then 30 seconds of silence 30 seconds of music 30 second of silence in this repeated on constantly for an hour and 20 minutes so I had to hang up with that person call them back. A nice and lady was assisted me and told me what was going on with my product saying it will be here Friday but my complaint with Amazon is this I was never given anything for my waste of time on the phone for an hour and 20 minutes it makes it really hard to keep Amazon prime when their service is so crappy like this I am going to delete my prime account because this makes no sense you do not keep a customer on hold for hour and 20 minutes and do not check in that is my complaint with Amazon as far as my fire stick I do like it I've only had it less than 24 hours there are no issues with my firestick as of yet if there is I will then make another review.

title: Pass if you have a choice
body: I got this as a prime day special to simply play with. It was $15 and then I had some discounts laying around so I basically got it free. I plan on using this on my next TV that I will add for my basement. Typically I use ROKU devices on all of my TV's.Pros and Cons....Pros:The Amazon Prime environment that is baked in is very good. The ability to use Prime Photos and do slideshows is probably it's best feature. You can also play some basic games, from the amazon app store like Crossy Road, etc. and they run just "ok". Kind of neat to play those kind of games on TV in a pinch if you're bored.Cons:The most important part, the apps and speed of streaming, by comparing this to a similar model Roku TV Stick, the Roku absolutely runs circles around the Fire Stick. The Roku is much faster to navigate, much much cleaner interface layout and the buffering of playing videos from any APP is much faster on the Roku. The Fire Stick I initially thought was just incapable of HD however I then realized it has an incredibly slow buffer, after about a minute or two of playing the video would finally clear up, which, if you use HULU with Live TV some something similar then that is not acceptable. My wireless and bandwidth speeds are not the issue either, both are very fast. Given those shortcomings, the price when on sale (prime day, black friday, cyber monday) is hard to beat, but if you're on the fence, go with the Roku.

title: Meh...Roku still better
body: I've purchased a few of these around the home to replace some aging Roku's. Initially, I was impressed with the ease of setup and the integrated remote to control my television and soundbar with the single remote. This is where my love affair ended unfortunately. After several months of consistent usage, we have issues on a daily basis. We are complete cord cutters and the stick seems like it's too underpowered to handle the streaming apps we use. Sling crashes at least once a day, Prime video w/HBO is hit or miss and the Alexa integration is pretty useless. It appears that as you open different apps on the device, the apps continue to run in the background as you navigate to other apps. You can see how after watching a show on Sling, then moving to Netflix, then Hulu and then Prime how this could be a potential problem. There doesn't appear to be a way close the apps out without actually restarting the stick manually. If these issues were relegated to a single stick I would certainly chalk it up to a corrupted device but all of them do this.Unfortunately, I've had to dust off the old Roku's, install and update them but the thing is they just work. Even as old as they are, they may be a bit slow but the apps work and they never crash which leads me to believe that the latest Roku's are that much better. I had higher expectations from the Fire Stick but in the end, the convenience of the single remote and ease of setup is not enough to keep me in fold.

title: FYI - Alexa / Voice Recognition DOES NOT WORK
body: The fire stick is slow to load when you first turn it or or switch to an app, but once you get it going its fine. Unfortunately some of the features don't work. At all. Ever. And most of the settings aren't "settings" that you can alter - they're just informational things. So its a problem when something isn't working. Like Alexa / Voice recognition. There is a small number of "Help" videos - none of them has ever addressed a problem I've had.Alexa / Voice recognition doesn't work for me. At all. Never has. I've reset the device by holding the home button. I've re-paired the remote. I've tried speaking loudy, slowly. I've tried every combination of button adn blue light. Nothing. Have I missed a step? I don't know. There aren't any instructions anywhere. This fact alone make me even more sure that mine JUST DOESNT WORK. There seem to be two camps. For one group it works perfectly with nearly zero effort. And then there is my group who get no results despite trying everything and scouring the internet for tips.I've also never had luck using the apps that lets you mirror or project your laptop or phone screen onto the TV via the firestick. So that another "feature" that has literally never, ever worked.The firestick works for streaming amazon prime - and other streaming services. But several of the other features touted by amazon simply don't. Its disappointing.

title: Maybe I'm just old school...
body: I agree about the set up and what the Fire Stick has to offer. Being an old school guy and not really into all the modern technology, I was surprised how fast I had this up and running. There is a learning curve to navigating through the different screens. My only issue was that the Fire remote will not turn off my TV. I can turn it on and do everything it's required to do, it just will not turn my TV off, I have to use the TV's original remote for that.I contacted Amazon support and spent way too much time with no positive result. The support Tech even had me hook up an Echo Dot I received from Amazon not to long ago. It was free from some other purchase so I did it. Folks...I'm not a fan of Alexa, the last thing I need is to have to talk to my TV. The Echo Dot did not fix my problems. Alexa could not turn the TV on or off, it kept telling me about some issue with why it wouldn't work that the support tech had no idea of. When a piece of equipment starts asking me questions and keeps repeating itself, it's time to call it quits. I was disappointed that a TV that is less than 6 months old isn't compatible with the remote. Two different support techs and over an hour of phone time didn't fix the remote issue. This includes a new second remote Amazon sent. Amazon tried to work out the bug but no luck. Over all I can live with the turning off issue but you all can have Alexa and it's eavesdropping capabilities.

title: No Alexa integration through AV receiver; Beware of unintentional ording of programming
body: I do not have any other Alexa devices. I connected the FireStick into an HDMI port on my AV receiver and then connected it to my home WiFi network. The FireStick/Alexa cannot change the AV receiver's HDMI inputs to choose the correct HDMI port for the FireStick (I have to do that through the AV receiver's remote) and while the sound for the programming through the FireStick does play properly through the AV receiver, Alexa cannot be contacted and does not reply. Basically, Alexa is completely non-existent when using the FireStick through my AV receiver. I searched through many options, settings and the help system and have been unable to find a solution.Streaming quality is good. A large selection of choices, but one must be VERY careful when perusing the programming selections as one can get charged for ordering something for additional cost without meaning to. There do not seem to be any popups asking you to confirm a purchase. Somehow I accidentally ordered PGA Live or some such nonsense at $9.95/month without knowing simply by hitting the wrong button at the wrong time. I canceled it the next day when I saw the email notification and charge, but haven't seen it credited back after two weeks. A popup saying "You are about to order XXXX. Do you want to continue" would be a good customer practice.Overall it is a passable, but not perfect, solution for additional programming.

title: There are kinks...
body: This gets mixed reviews from me. I was using another device to stream Amazon Prime on my television and received notification that Amazon was no longer going to allow it after the end of September so I basically had no choice but to get the fire stick. I never had an issue with that device but I can't say the same about the fire stick. When switching the TV input on my television between cable and the fire stick, the screen often remains black. I then have to play with the buttons on the fire remote to get it to turn on. There is no ON/OFF light indicator so it is impossible to know if it is on or off and to make matters worse, there is a delay so did I just turn it on or off? Who knows. One time it refused to turn on at all and I had to unplug and replug the stick into the tv itself. (I've only had it about a month.) Unplug the cord. Take out the batteries from the remote and put them back in. Have no idea why it wasn't working but after 20 frustrating minutes it turned back on so great. Which leads me to another issue - WHY did they design the remote so opening the back to put in & replace the batteries is such a chore?? It is like a puzzle box. The last issue is with the voice control. I tried to use it but our echo dot responded - from another room where my kid was sleeping. Not helpful. I didn't realize the voice control has to work through an echo device, I assumed it was independent.

title: Ok for the price. Not great.
body: I already have a Apple TV in my living room and wanted a streaming box for my bedroom. I decided to save some money and get the fire stick which was more than a hundred dollars cheaper. I have to say I was disappointed in the fire stick.Pros:Very low price.Has a decent amount of apps (Not as many as Apple).Alexa integration if you need that. (I don’t use it)Cons:Clunky operating system.Hard to find apps you purchased.Several Amazon apps preloaded and cant be deleted.Amazon apps displayed first.No way to rearrange apps to your preference.Apps continually log out requiring you to go online and reactivate over and over again.Apps clunky compared to Apple TV.Have to constantly restart apps and device due to freezing.WI-FI only. No option for wired connection.If you don’t have blazing wi-fi, expect the dreaded buffering spinning ball often.If you are looking for a ridiculously low priced streaming box with Amazon prime and Alexa integration, and you have fast wi-fi, or are ok with buffering, and don’t mind having to work a little to get the box to work, then the fire stick is for you.As for me, the fire stick has been ok for awhile, but I think I’m going to go ahead and spend the extra money and get another Apple TV. You get what you pay for.

title: Smart but no Genius
body: I bought this Fire TV Stick to enhance the failing IQ of an aging Samsung Smart TV. It has been useful and is easy to use, although I don’t use the Alexa voice command functions. It has not been very useful, though, at integrated my Apple TV subscription or my Peacock TV subscription, and I have been unable to find much help with my issues. It was easy enough to download the AppleTV app, but it keeps giving me messages that in-app purchases are not allowed whenever I try to watch anything. In short, it does not seem to recognize that I have an AppleTV subscription. I can start something on my tablet and then watch on my television, but that’s a step that should be unnecessary. There is no log-in function in the Amazon AppleTV app of the sort you find with Netflix, which could mitigate the problem. I have been unable to even find NBC’s new Peacock TV streaming service. It was a modest investment, and for watching Amazon Video, Netflix, and a few other streaming service I use, it has been useful. I figure updates will be timely and seamless, so I am not dissatisfied, just somewhat disappointed, especially at the difficulty in finding documentation or support. (I finally found advice to call for support, but after having spent way too much time with automated voice systems, I didn’t call. I should be able to find written instructions, or engage in a live chat.)

title: Don’t buy if you want to use it with your echo dot
body: Alexa never had trouble with my FireTV Edition Insignia TV, but God forbid I try to do anything with this Firestick (which I bought when my TV screen went black after an update to Amazon software, costing me already ($250). I already blame amazon for that.Instead of playing things on Hulu when I tell it to play whatever, it just searches for it and pulls it up. Doesn’t play it “here’s what I found). It will not sync with Alexa Dot to turn my tv on and off, volume control, or anything else that is helpful that I grew accustomed to.So to recap, my TV crapped out before a year passed by when I updated Amazon software (unfortunately I bought it at Best Buy and lost my receipt and they can’t find it by serial number because with all their technology, they don’t use it to benefit customers), I can barely use my Dot to control it, which is the majority of the reason I use amazon products in the first place. I would not recommend this device to anyone.Oh- and don’t even bother with screensaver settings because it basically turns the screen black all of 30 seconds after it starts no matter how long or what you do with the settings.I would not recommend this product without significant improvements. Also, the cost to replace the remote shouldn’t be almost the same as the entire device. That’s just ridiculous.

title: Started great
body: We got a fire stick just because it was a good deal and wanted to test it. It works great! Sometimes there may be a slight streaming issue, but it's only happened twice, and for only a few seconds. What we really like it the overwhelming amount of content. We thought there would be limited options, but there is always something to watch for free (even moreso with Prime). Our little one found a favorite with Dino Dana on Prime, and it's been a great find to increase her dinosaur love. Overall good product. We don't use voice recognition that often so can't really rate that. It's worth owning one, especially if you find a good deal. Ours was $24 I think? Worth every penny.Edit 8 months later: Not sure what happened, but the fire stick seems barley usable now. Nothing has changed with our setup, but after streaming for about 5-10 "slow internet connection" pops up and can't watch anything. In settings it shows our connection is good and strength good. Tried at different times of day and Everytime same thing. Even deleted apps and clear caches, but still problem persists. This has gone beyond annoyance to a severely bad problem. If we can't stream something what's the point of using? I had to drop my review considerably due to instances. Are there any suggestions to make this thing function as intended now?

title: Overheats all the time !!!
body: At $30, the firetv stick packs a tons of features and it is really easy to install/use. My biggest issue is with the device overheating ALL THE TIME !! It overheated within 15 mins of my first use, and has since been struggling to maintain at a reasonable/operational temperature. My cabinet is designed to house all my media/entertainment needs - it has cable TV box, Blu-ray player, PS4 consoles, WiFi hub, charging stations for laptop/phones etc. None of these other devices has ever experienced any overheating or air flow issue, until the firetv stick arrived. For a product that sells millions in qty and is designed by a huge corporation, I am baffled by how the firetv design got approved with such a constant and obvious issue. Just look up the website to see how many customers/users are experiencing this. If you want to have a smooth operation of this firetv stick, be prepared to invest another $15-$20 on some heat removal solutions (addition of heat sink or heat fins, plus a small fan or blower) to increase the air flow and heat dissipation ability around the firetv stick. I would have gladly paid $50 for a firetv stick that is designed with a better cooling solution and works well from the get-go, instead of $30 for a device which I then have to add some must-have accessories to make it work properly.

title: So-so
body: Amazon practically forced me to buy this product because they were going to suspend the ability of my Blu-Ray player to stream Prime Videos. The set-up was not too difficult. However there were two serious issues once I started to use the Fire Stick:(1) The voice recognition simply refuses to recognize the phrase "Alexa, find Amazon Prime Video." Likewise, "Alexa, open Amazon Prime Video" did not work. The same for "Alexa, open my Prime Watchlist." Her answer: "I have several alternatives for that." But none of them included Prime Video! Instead, idiotic alternatives were offered by Alexa: "Prime Time." Or some children's stories. Or Amazon Music. To open Prime Video, I had to install the Silk Browser and use the TV as a computer to open the website. Later, however, when I turned on the Fire Stick app, it went directly to a menu that was easy to navigate.(2) Once I was able to open the Prime Video website and watch a video, the picture kept blurring at random times, as though the video screen was being smeared by a gooey liquid. This never happens with the antenna-TV shows, and it never happened with the Blu-Ray player streaming videos. Now, this happened with the first two high-definition TV shows I watched. It did not happen on my next viewing, which was a low-definition movie.

title: Defective remote and consent mishaps
body: The fire stick itself booted up fine. The remote did not work at all. I changed batteries. Still did not work. Called Amazon and a technician helped me over the phone and together we rebooted the TV, the Firestick and when she wanted to take control of the TV/ Firestick an icon appeared on the TV asking me to accept the technician and of course I could not do that because the fire stick did not work. She said she would send a new remote. A cable guy came for another reason and offered to look at the remote and found a "pin" in the battery bay was bent. He fixed it. Great. So, yesterday I got delivery of the new "remote" and what I received was a new Firestick. In the meantime, the original remote only works some time. I was looking forward to the new remote so I could return the defective one. I called again and a technician made me go through the same steps and tried to add an app to my phone which did not work because you need a working remote to pair them. She then said a new remote could not be sent until I returned the second Firestick. By the way, I was sent a "refurbished" Firestick when my original was new. I waited 8 days to get the first replacement now probably will wait over 2 weeks while they wait for the return and then send me a working remote. Not happy

title: Update - not staying connected to  WiFi G network.  Works fine, Amazon heavy interface
body: April 2021 updateThe issues with staying connected to WiFi still exist and seems to be getting worse. Bad unit? A more wide spread issue? Don’t know.=============================================Does everything you want a streaming device to do. Replacing an old Roku, so old that Roku offered me a $15 deal on a new device. Why didn’t I take that deal?3/15 update - still using the ReCast/Fire stick combo. The ReCast has been solid and a good purchase. The Fire stick has started developing issues. It doesn’t want to stay connected to our WiFi G network. Everything else in the house has no issues so I can only guess it’s the stick . There’s no way to troubleshoot so I can only guess.BTW, it connects fine to the slower network. Yes, same WiFi access point.===================================We recently cut the cord but my wants her DVR features. The simplest way to make that happen was a ReCast and Fire stick. And it works. DVR for live tv and the Fire stick for streaming from multiple services.I’m not sure if the interface is from the Fire stick (I think it is) or the ReCast but it’s very Amazon centric. It would be nice to dial that down a bit. Really Amazon, I know you’re out there 🤨

title: Good while it lasts ...updated replacement sent
body: I received the FireTVstick from Amazon during the first week of December 2019. Setup was easy and because it came from Amazon some of the setup was already done. It worked until the second week of March. It no longer loads and is stuck on the fireTVstick logo.I have done online chats no one knows how how to fix it. I have made phone calls and was told that technicians were working on it. They called a week later to ask more questions most I had answered previously. I asked if they could replace the firestick and was told the warranty expired at the beginning of March. On the order page It still says that I may receive a replacement until Dec. 2020. I guess they are not keeping their word. I have never had a problem with Amazon before this. I am extremely disappointed with Amazon and this product.Went through the process of formally asking for a replacement. Waiting to hear from Amazon.—-Update—-After not hearing from technical support for a week, I contacted them again. I was asked to preform another reset. When this failed the support put me on hold for about a minute, then asked for a shipping address for them to send me a refurbished stick.It has been working for about one month with no major issues. Hoping it continues to work

title: Steams don't recover when connection is poor
body: I admit, my internet connection isn't the best, but it's how the Fire TV Stick reacts when connection is poor that causes my frustration. My internet/router/modem seems to have an issue where all of my devices aren't able to load any data from the internet for a brief period of time; somewhere between 15 to 30 seconds. All devices indicate they're still connected but there's no data transferring in that time. When the connection restores, all of my devices resume back to normal, but when using any streaming service, the device will freeze the image, goes to black, displays an error message and never recovers. Even after my other devices are back to normal, the stream does not restore. Even after changing channels/streams, going to the Fire Stick home screen, exiting the streaming app and relaunching the app, the. steam. does. not. recover... I have to unplug the power supply to the fire stick and wait for it to reboot for it to reconnect to my internet. This has occurred on Netflix, Direct TV Now and SlingTV so the app developers are not likely on the hook to fix this issue. Tried the HDMI extender cord, no improvement either. Firmware and app versions are all up to date. Possibly an update is needed to ensure the device is versatile with all internet connection types.

title: Really good but not perfect
body: Its a great piece of technology and is essential for even most "smart TV's". Smart televisions have libraries of program applications from which users download to their TV's. The problem is that a great many popular apps do not appear in these smart TV libraries. Firestick also has this problem, although its library of program apps is extensive and growing. Firestick uses a method of providing content found in some missing TV apps by offering connections thru Amazon Prime (with add-on subscription rates). These Prime channels do not provide the full programming options of the stand-alone app. For example, the Britbox service on Amazon Prime is not as complete as the standalone app loaded to our other streaming devices. And even though I am paying the Britbox subscription fee and have the app loaded to 4 other streaming devices, watching Britbox on the Firestick would require a second paid subscription with Amazon Prime. There are many other channels/apps I use on my other devices (mostly sports related) that are not available to download and install to the Firestick. Notwithstanding the programming limitations, the Firestick is an excellent streaming device that is easy to manage and use. Its great for travel and for connecting a guest room TV when company comes.

title: It has issues
body: Update: 09/15/19 : as soon as I posted the review, amazon was contacting me. So they have great customer service and it turned out that the issue I was having was because of the hdmi extender. Which is still really annoying. The stick seems to be working for now. But I’m keeping and eye on it.It would be nice to find a hdmi splitter that doesn’t affect the one product though.Upgraded from 1 star to 3.Original:I have had the fire TV stick for about two months. I am disappointed. First in did not arrive when it originally said it would, but thats fine. Most of my problems are in the start up. Its constantly restarting itself. It gets to the main menu and shuts down and starts to reboot. This occurs almost every time we go to watch something. It probably restarts 4 or 5 times before I either give up or it works. Once it starts to work its usually fine, however sometimes while in the middle of watching something it cuts out and shuts down and reboots again. This has happened a handful of times as well. I know its not my wifi, because I recently had it fixed, and I have checked and troubleshooted the problem with no luck. I think I might have just gotten a dud, because I have friends who have it and they have no issues at all.

title: Disney Plus won't launch on it
body: Bought this for viewing Disney Plus. And now, after having it for about two weeks, it suddenly won't launch Disney Plus. (We did watch Disney Plus at first, for about a week.)I've gone through all the steps recommended by Disney Plus help line. Tried to contact Firestick help line and of course they don't want a person to talk to you. Reviewed the steps they offer online for troublshooting and it was the same as what the Disney Plus support person said. Did the unplug and replug -- didn't work. Did the sign out and sign back in. Did a total factory reset, which required reinstalling all the apps. Didn't work. They say "wait and see" if the problem resolves itself. Yeah right. My Disney plays on other platforms, my Ipad and desktop computer, but not on firestick.To be fair, other things work wonderfully on Firestick. Amazon prime videos work really well. I like the set up, the display, the remote-- just about everything. Firestick would get five stars and more if it would launch Disney Plus.From the forums I've visited and from other searches it seems many others have had issues with Disney Plus on this device as well. What's Up? How can they let this screw up go on for days and days?

title: Room for improvement but does the job.
body: Had fairly frequent buffering issues causing the recording to pause for several seconds. This would also occur whenever the microwave was on. I resolved this by adding a dual-band router to my network, I recommend this for anyone using the wireless router provided by your internet provider. No buffering issues at all now but I have a new problem, I can not delete recordings using my primary Fire Stick 4k, it's like it completely ignores my selection of Delete and jumps back to my options (all other commands work). I can work around this in two ways, I can go to my other TV that uses the basic Fire Stick, it can delete no problem. Also I can use Alex to delete but it doesn't work for some recordings saying it can't find it. The channel guide is nice, however from time-to-time when I select a program to record it doesn't mark it as selected, weird. This is all inconvenient but for the money I'm saving grabbing free TV from the airwaves it's worth it to me. I use my Harmony remote (integration is very nice) so I can't rate the one that comes with the Fire Stick (only used it briefly for testing). Overall I'm happy with the Fire Sticks and Recast devices. I'll be thrilled when the delete problem goes away.

title: Promissing but Frustrating Compared to Roku
body: So my only experience before this has been with Roku so I have to compare to that. I had to get it to use as display for the Fire Recast DVR. The first thing I notice is that it's much more cluttered than Roku. Where Roku is quick and easy to find things, this one is cluttered and hard to sort through. I have to click 3-5x more times to do something.The voice assistant is a definite advantage, or at least would be if it weren't utterly incompetent. I try to go to my recordings and it works maybe 20% of the time, the rest of the time it searches for a TV show called "recordings", "guide", or whatever else so I know very well it's understood me. It's frustrating to say the least.There are some streaming shows I found more easily with Firestick which is nice. I also like the browser and VPN options I can choose from so that's good. However, the Firestick has fewer choices overall it seems. There's also no way to mirror the screen on my Windows 10 laptop to the screen which was a killer feature on even the cheap Roku Express. So overall it's not an awful product and the remote is of good quality, but I still like my Roku better for anything I don't have to use this for.

title: It doesn't understand me
body: I got this new FireStick because my old fire stick was several years old and friends said I should get a new one which would be much faster, downloading etc. Well, I don't know if it's faster --- probably, I'm usually in no hurry. I have Alexa on my new cell phone and now on my new Firestick. Neither one seems to understand me. I ordinary am a fast talker and even took special 'diction' classes (in the drama department) in college - several decades ago. Still, the few times I have attempted to slow down and actually use Alexa - it comes up with something totally alien to what I had said --- so, I have decided to not try to use it anymore. As much as I dislike having to spell stuff out (on Amazon Prime Video searches) at least I know the search engine will understand what I type in. Maybe I needed a new Firestick and am glad I got this one on sale (for the price of the next level down). I didn't have any high hopes for Alexa after so-so success with my phone - so I didn't count it into the cost of this item. If you are used to Alexa and other new-fangled voice activated stuff (I'm 66 and not into most high-tech stuff anyways) then hopefully you will be more successful with the voice functions.

title: Frustrated with accessing brit box on prime with the firestick
body: I wanted to send an email to amazon rather than writing a review, but couldn't find a way to do that, so I am writing a review (the chat option didn't even seem to work). We bought this firestick because HULU wasn't using Sony blue ray player anymore and we wanted to watch CBS access on our tv and not just our television. We decided we wanted to go back to brit box on amazon prime so we could watch the latest season of Father Brown and could not find it with the firestick- signing up with britbox was not an option. Our daughter told us she knew it was on brit box on prime because she was watching it! When we tried it through our blue-ray player, sure enough we could do it. I think we had to go on the computer to add britbox to our subscription. I have been very frustrated- the way the firestick view on the television is not easy to navigate, and alexa seems to be useless if I ask her to find something for CBS. I think I saw that Amazon prime is not longer going to be accessed through the sony player and am wondering how hard it is going to be for me to access the Britbox.The bottom line is it seems like amazon could make their prime videos a lot more easy to navigate and access.

title: Volume Doesn't Play Nice with Harmony
body: Our Den has a Fire TV Cube and a Harmony Hub. This combination has worked very well as Alexa has no trouble telling harmony to reconfigure our entertainment equipment to the right sources (and turn it on/off). Once the TV is on, 99% of what we want to do works off of the Fire TV remote. The one thing we can't do (well) is change the volume. Alexa will adjust it using harmony, but in relatively large increments so I end up picking up the Harmony remote for this one task.On Prime Day, this package was cheaper than the (not discounted) "with volume" remote (only) so I bought this box for the remote. Pairing was easy. Unfortunately, once paired, the remote only wanted to work off of the IR blasters in the Cube. The cube was totally hidden because we have a harmony (and wanted to keep it this way). I pulled an extra blaster out of my closet (maybe the original Fire TV one) and tried to locate it inconspicuously, but there isn't a good place to put it so it really doesn't work.It's unfortunate that Alexa is smart enough to use the harmony for volume, but the Fire TV remote is not. Otherwise, I could put the Harmony remote away and give this system 5*.

title: Inconsistent functionality makes us rely on hard resets
body: Our family has owned this device since July 2019 (approximately 3 months) with unfortunately multiple issues. We owned a Roku prior to this and were very pleased with it. However, we decided to try a Fire Stick during a Prime Day deal. It has met some of our expectations. It does pair nicely with our TV so that we only need to use 1 remote control. It also was a simple set-up that offers sufficient parental controls and user friendly interface. The reason we feel the need to provide an overall 3 star review is that our Fire Stick consistently freezes. We’ve had approximately a dozen instances over the last few months where we have paused the programming we were watching and when we go back to continue viewing the only buttons that work on the remote are the volume functions. The only fix is a hard reset of unplugging the Fire Stick from the TV and plugging it back in. We’ve never had issues like this with our Amazon products so unfortunately we may have gotten a faulty piece of equipment. If we didn’t have to perform hard resets consistently we would certainly give this a 5 star review as it is practical and seemless, enough for our 3 year old to use and understand.

title: Started off well....
body: I have owned the Fire Stick for 24 hours now and I might have to return it for the newer 4k one. I have been a long time user of Roku's (every TV in the house has one and I have a spare just in case one dies). But a service that I subscribe too does not have an app for Roku (its been in development for over a year now) but there is one available for the Fire Stick. So, I broke down and purchased one last night. Everything was going fine with set up until I got to the registration part; it kept giving me an internet connection error when I typed in my password. I had to switch to another available WiFi channel which was smooth sailing after that and I was able to stream using the Fire stick until an hour ago. The stream stopped in the middle of a show that I was watching. I immediately thought I had a WiFi connection issue but none of my other wireless devices were having connection problems. So, I decided to check the same show on the same app on my Roku and surprise no connection or playback issues. I'll attempt to get the Fire Stick working again by switching it back to the original WiFi channel. If that doesn't not work its going back to the store.

title: Navigation, replay
body: I hope some developers will hear my complaints/problems using this device. 1. Navigation. e.g. I am watching a series and want to go back to a previous episode. In some cases, I manage to do this. In other cases, the only way is forward to the next episode. I spent a lot of time screaming, "No, I don't want that one. I want to go back," at Mr. Robot, for example, and am currently having the same trouble with the FrIench TV series Origins. That's just one I can remember. It has happened multiple times, though not always. 2. Since I have watched all 6 episodes of the French shocunit show Origins, it seems my only option is to watch episode 6 again. There is another series also called Origins, and the device will direct me only to seasons and episodes of the series I don't want. That's after I have clicked on the one I do want. I have had similar trouble when a movie and a series had the same title. I have tried vocal prompts like, "Play Origins with [actor's name]. Alexa says there is no such thing. And "Play [episode title] after I have looked it up online. Alexa plays a song with a similar title. In 8 years I have NEVER had this trouble with Netflix.

title: Better design than older version, ability to voice command is convenient.
body: Before I tell you the pros and cons of this newer fire stick, here is the short back story on the older one: I bought an Amazon Fire stick years ago, and finally hooked it up a few months ago. I procrastinated thinking I would be too much of a tech idiot to do it, but it wasn't hard at all. We used it on the living room tv for a while. It was kind of annoying the way it was designed (with the cord sticking out of the side of the stick) because it was a very tight fit the way my HDMI ports were situated on my tv.When I saw that a newer version with voice recognition was on sale on Black Friday, I jumped on the deal. I'm glad I did.PROS:The fire stick now comes with a small extender cord which helps if your tv ports are designed at an awkward place, like mine are.The ability to hold down a button and say, "Rewind 2 minutes" is convenient. I used to have trouble going too far back when I would try to rewind. It's nice to be able to give an exact time.You don't have to press a lot of buttons if you like the voice recognition. For instance, you can pick up the remote, hold down the microphone button, and say, "Play Criminal Minds on Netflix" and it does.This newer version of the Fire Stick also has volume up and down buttons and a mute button. No need to pick up the tv remote at all. The power button turns your tv on and off as well. When you are setting up your Fire Stick, you will set the remote down near the tv, and it will magically sync.If you can wait until Black Friday to buy one, they are usually only $19.99 then.It has some free apps built in (IMDB, Philo, Crackle). Netflix and Hulu require a subscription, but the Fire Stick is a one-time purchase, there is no continual cost for that.CONS:The voice recognition is a little picky. You will want to learn to use specific phrases. If you want to start a show from the beginning, you need to say, "Play from beginning", "Start this show over" does not work. Think strong verbs. Rewind, fast forward, play seem to be the phrases it likes.There is a lag (quite long on Hulu) when you tell it to rewind or play from beginning. It will continue to play for a minute or so before it actually executes the command.Whatever you are watching will not stay paused for long before it goes back to the Fire Stick home screen. I find that annoying to have to go back in to a show. I am spoiled to DVDs which stay paused indefinitely. I have actually realized I would rather look at the clock when I am getting up to do something, and then look at the clock when I get back, and use the voice command to tell it to go back that many minutes (the amount I've missed.)A Fire Stick does require Wi-fi. I know most people have wi-fi in their homes now, but if you don't, you will want to get a router. My mother wanted to get a Fire Stick eventually. She talked about it for a while. Like I said, I took advantage of the Black Friday sale, and then realized, "Oh, %&*#, she doesn't have wi-fi." For a router, I searched on Amazon, but ended up ordering one from the most popular big box chain store in the USA (I usually don't like to give them my business, but it was the best deal on a well-rated product I could find.) I paid less than $30 for a router which has been working well for the purpose.

title: Excellent media streamer for $20
body: Picked one up before the holidays as it was on sale for $20. Hard not to love it just for that.Arrived, and it came pre-configured to both my Amazon account, as well as my Wi-Fi (very convenient - or very scary). clearly had gotten that info from my Alexa devices - even though I had explicitly said not to obtain that information.Once connected to TV and running...Many reviewers say that it is very Amazon centric. And Yes, it favors services provided by Amazon as it default to those when starting up. Minor inconvenience.Prime and Netflix - likely main reason people by this gizmo are good apps. Amazon music app is cool, the YouTube app is fantastic and one could spend a lifetime watching content from YT. There are tons of apps for utilities and one would wish for a few news apps (CBS is there and a good stream).Remote is RF and hence does not need line of sight. Handy - as if you have an AV setup, you can hide the FireStick inside the cabinet, attached to a HDMI port on your AV receiver. Works well.What does not work that well is Alexa integration. Many have complained that FireStick cannot be "seen" from Echo units. Hence, I cannot say "Alexa, turn on Sony", or "Alexa, switch input", etc, etc as I only getting a "the hub that device connected to is not responding". Does not matter if TV is on or not. All my Alexa devices are on the same 2.4 GHz network, same subnet, dedicated to just IoT stuff here at home.Still, minor inconvenience but it WOULD be cool to be able to turn TV on with voice (and TV allows for turn-on via HDMI-CEC control as it works when other devices sending the signal to the TV)."the hub that device connected to is not responding" topic is obviously well known and there are tons of comments and suggestions online. None of them working. Why would the FireStick not be visible to an Echo device when both are on the same network, both are on, and (obviously) both are connected to the internet, live, active, etc. Baffles me.All this said; I wish people understood how powerful and flexible these streamers are, and stopped buying "Smart" TVs and instead only picked up a dumb display to connect peripherals to. Why? The built-in apps in TVs often stop seeing regular SW updates after a few years, potentially rendering the smart TV really dumb, forcing add-on pieces like a streamer, or just a total replacement. This when a well supported media streamer could have done the job better from day one, at a lower cost (both to purchase as well as to upgrade if suddenly no longer supported).

title: ALL THREE WORKED FINE OUT OF THE BOX, BUT THERE'S MORE TO THE STORY... especially if using YouTubeTV
body: We've had a legacy FireStick for years, just for Netflix. It's pretty basic, but no problems. During that time we had SAT service, then a big name Cable TV hookup, to four big screen sets. After cost and other issues led us to bury the dish, then cut the cable, we tried YouTubeTV and loved it (especially the customizable "live" user interface). So we bought three of these "2019-build" Gen 2 FireSticks.NOTE: This FireStick is actually years-old hardware with a new Remote that can control TV On-Off, Volume, Mute. That's nice, but the description seems to imply the FireStick is newer than it is. OK, just be aware.Setup was a snap. I downloaded and installed apps for YouTubeTV, Netflix, HBO NOW, SiriusXM, Internet Speed Test, etc. We have extremely robust 200+ Mbps broadband and a powerful AC2600-class wireless router. The 5 GHz band is used for the FireSticks and their speed test apps consistently measured Download at 100+ Mbps on three, and 60+ Mbps on the other, with extremely low Ping and Jitter. However, I soon noticed YouTubeTV was streaming at only 720p, not 1080p as advertised. I checked settings, restarted FireSticks, etc., without success. So I contacted YouTubeTV tech support, which was great...very professional and thorough. Together we did some deep troubleshooting, including sending screen shots of geek data...streaming stats for their app, Version info, and how YouTubeTV streamed on other devices (tablets, phones, PCs...all at 1080p). YouTubeTV spent a lot of time trying to solve the resolution anomaly. Then I had an idea...I bought a new 4K FireStick and quickly found it streamed YouTubeTV at 1080p on all four TVs! So this regular "1080p" FireStick doesn't deliver that on YouTubeTV but the newer design "4K" FireStick does so easily. Clearly there's a problem but, in all fairness to Amazon, this FireStick does stream another service (Amazon Prime Movies) at 1080p. Also, though my three FireSticks were by that time past the Return cutoff, Amazon gave me nearly full credit toward three new 4K FireSticks, now on order.Always keep your eye on the Return cutoff date because once that door closes it's hard to get in touch with a live person at Amazon. I searched their web site and finally got to a Call-Back request page, which was really fast. My phone rang within seconds and wait time was a few more seconds. Apparently not many people made it through the maze! After I receive the 4K FireSticks and use them for some time, I'll document the experience with them.

title: HD..Not 4K. CAN'T TELL THE DIFFERENCE
body: I got the newest 4K streaming stick with alexa voice control remote. As of yet i haven't purchased a 4K T.V. but the firestick works on any high def T.V. with a hdmi port. There are lots of free movie and T.V. apps so you can cut the cord and save lots of $$ doing away with cable. You will just have to simply connect a OTA antenna to the back of your TV to receive your local channels. The remote is ok but not great. It does sync easily with your T.V.s CEC control and lets you use the remote for T.V. power on and off. Volume control and alexa voice command. But personally i found it just as quick and easy to manually navigate the apps you want to watch rather than using the button on the remote to ask for a certain app. More times than not the voice control will get what your asking it wrong. But the volume control and power control on the remote is a very good plus over the basic remote. The battery compartment for remote is a little tricky to get opened at first but if you just apply a slight pressure at the same time as sliding the compartment down jt will slide right open. And getting to the streaming quality it will depend on which app you are using. Surprising to me is that the Amazon Prime Video app uses a lot more wifi data than most of the other apps making the shows and movies buffer quite a bit sometimes. I am curious why Amazon Prime uses so much more data vs Netflix or Hulu for example but it surely does. And if you have a Roku device and are used to the Rokus functions it will take a bit of getting used to how the Firestick apps are downloaded and stored compared to the Roku. But they both have equally good and bad comparisons when it comes to their individual functions. Both have excellent picture and sound. What i would really like is for Amazon's firestick to have a remote control app for your android or ios phone. Roku does have the free remote app to download and works great navigating and also has alexa voice control and tou can plug earphones into your phone for private listening. And also remember when buying a firestick to check the gift box if it is going to be used by someone else in their home. If you check the connect to my account box instead all of your information on your other firesticks will be on the new one and also the Amazon buying app with your personal information. So be vigilante and at least have a PIN set up on your Amazon account so no non approved purchases can be made.

title: So far so good for non-techy AFTER some tweaks to imrove buffering.
body: So, OK, my "dumb" Samsung from 2014 - doesn't have a lot of ports etc. Had rigged up an extra-long HDMI cable to my Amazon Kindle Fire for a while just to watch Amazon Prime. So figured, what the heck, with this BF deal.Took me about 3-4 hours to install - for one thing LOL I only got by accident that the big circle in the middle of the remote is a button of a kind. Srsly I am used to BUTTONS not disks on a remote that u kinda have to know. After going through the tedium of setting up accounts, re a test run with Disney Plus the dreaded blue swirl of slow buffering. Or whatever.Yeesh, I hate tinkering with tech stuff. But luv the Mandalorian and my Amazon Prime more. And I have an old router etc. so problems aren't unexpected. Looked up some Googled articles and sure enough, u can go into ur preferences menu via the remote and turn off stuff like data monitoring (look for the online article by techjunkie). I think I stopped Alexa shopping and some other stuff. 'Coz the stick comes with pre-installed stuff, and even instructions say too many apps can affect performance.That worked, or maybe everyone got off the Internet at once. Anyway my needs are extremely modest and I hate messing with this kind of stuff. But got the Mandalorian Chapter 3 in glorious detail - don't know what is about Amazon Prime but the picture is AWESOME for Good Omens, movies etc. So far so good, and I know I need some upgrades. Sad to say the little thing got me hooked, I'll probably get more Amazon tech stuff. Hopefully the battery thing for the remote isn't as bad as some reviews. Way better than hooking up a 10ft HDMI cable every time u want to watch Prime on the TV.Suggestions for Amazon - Not everyone knows about single big button buttons. Put in diagram. I thought the install had froze at the language screen and only figured out button by accident. Put suggestions about turning off stuff or ability to de-install in - like I personally have no need for Game Stop whatever that is. Have pity on ur non-tech, don't care about all the electronic gizmo customers. Tho it is not Amazon's fault some of this stuff, or bad weather affecting 'net etc.

title: Its ok - but
body: Only had this item for about a week. But since Amazon sent me an email asking me to review it here are my observations so far.Cons -It needs to plug into the wall and does not plug into my TVs USB like my chromecast.It has no lights on the remote making it hard to use in the darkThe voice button is too close to the power button and too often I shut the TV off instead of calling up AlexaAlexa will not listen to me all the time, it appears to have an very limited selection of commandsAlexa has a learning curve - My Wife could hear me shouting at Alexa from the other room as she would not do what I asked, I found out you did not have to say Alexa and you had to hold the voice button down.The home screen is cluttered and hard to figure out what I want to do. For example I wish to find all the Amazon movies I bought and it is buried somewhere in the menu. I would suggest a my movies button showing just my library of movies.The search button brings up movies versions I did not buy and it was not clear which was the one I did buy, I had to open each file to find You Own thisSet up is slow talking about 40 minutes to get fully set upPros -All of Amazon is at my finger tipsIt works fastStreaming works wellEasy to set up.Very AffordableDelivers what it promises to doConnected and controls the TV with out any effort and reduces the need for the second remote (And if I am lucky will totally replace the remote, but I am not sure yet)So now I have a Choromecast, Roku and a fire stick. Not sure which one I like the best, I think each one has it's pros and cons. But Amazon just notified me that are discontinuing support for WII U, our favorite streaming device, so we need another option to watch Amazon shows and the Google Comcast is no use in this area.If you know what you want to watch this works well, if not it has 101 choices.And who know what I will discover in the next few weeks of using this product, I might even overcome some of negatives just by understanding this complicated thing more.

title: Works well, but...
body: I've had a Roku 3 for several years now (and the original Roku before that). I'm in the process of cord cutting and needed an additional streaming device so that all the TV's in the house are able to access Sling and YouTube TV. I originally planned to get another Roku, but they were all backordered after the holiday and I didn't want to wait 2 weeks, so thought I'd give the Fire TV Stick a try. I'd have to say that overall it works well, and I'm very pleased with the performance and picture quality, but there are few things that make me like it less than my trusty Roku 3.1. Doesn't work with my Logitech Harmony 900 remote because it is Bluetooth only, so I had to put it in a different room than I was planning (where I have Harmony Hub), and move the Roku to the room with the Harmony 900.2. Initial setup was smooth except for picking the apps to initially install. The setup wizard didn't allow scrolling through the list, so only had a choice of a handful of apps instead of the full list. Figuring out how to install the apps after the initial setup was much harder than it should have been - not where I would expect to find it in the UI and it took half an hour to figure out how to do it. Not to mention, that every app I installed caused me to get immediately spammed with email about it, as well as again a few days later asking me to rate/review each app.3. The Menu screen is organized sub-optimally, with initial focus being on a menu bar at the top that is almost worthless other than for getting to the Settings screen, which is the farthest away from the initial menu selection. Below the worthless menu is space for an ad, which takes up about 1/3 of the screen. So every time I come to the Menu screen I have to click down button at least twice to get from the menu, to the ad, to the Recent Item, or an additional time to get to the Your Apps and Channels row. I don't mind seeing an ad, but please don't make it dominate the screen and force me to navigate through it every time.

title: Excellent streaming device.
body: I got the Fire TV Stick to use on an old dumb TV after using the first gen chromecast since it came out. I had been curious about switching to Fire TV, but the deal breaker was still needing another remote for the TV. I only recently noticed that the remote now has an IR blaster, so decided to give it a shot.The only reason I am knocking a star off is because the Prime Video interface is not as well designed as the Android phone counterpart. Several times while watching a series on Prime, resume will loose track of what episode it left off of when waking the device, and replay episodes already seen despite the progress bars showing they have already finished. Also, I don't know if I am just not looking in the right places, but I can not find where to adjust the power saving feature (preferably set it for longer), which can be an annoyance when you have a show paused for too long and it enters into power save mode, thus taking you back to the home screen and potentially the aforementioned issue. There appears to only be the option to set how long until the screensaver comes on. That said, they are just minor quirks which could potentially be ironed out in an update, I still prefer the Fire TV stick over the chromecast, and I really like when a channel from Sling appears on the home screen they show what is currently airing and how far through it is. The mobile Fire TV app is also fantastic for when you need a keyboard.As far as Alexa, I really wasn't planning on using it, but given how few buttons there are I figured I would give it a try and it does make navigating the Fire TV a whole lot easier. You may also use it to switch inputs on the TV too, but although granted it is outside the scope of the remote it would be nice to be able to change the channel on the Antenna tuner too thus completely eliminating the need of a second remote. I also like the fact that it only listens while holding the button. Other than that, I don't use any IoT devices, so that is the extent of my utilization.

title: Great stick, easy to setup works with my RCA tv, yay , but  with two annoyaces.
body: Says it comes programmed with your account but it does not !... it was a pain in the arse to enter my long random password.. this was the only drawback.It came up good and connected right away with my WiFi.Programming the remote to talk to the TV was easy and quick; however two complaints I have with the remote...1) does not have a "entry selector" for the TV, so I still have to use the tv remote to switch the input2) remote is not lighted, so..... I like to watch TV in the dark, but not so much that I memorize the buttons;3) the rounded shape of the remote is kinda slippery...Good point of the remote, being bluetooth I don't need to point it to the stick.Another annoyance that I find is that there is no setup to show only "Included with Prime" or "set spending limits"Everything else, looks great. It loads fast, and have decent image.**** However...... review of the review...... something funky happens with the volume that changes up and down, not much but it goes into more quiet and then goes back higher before returning to the normal set level. Not sure what is the problem.. I swapped to another HDMI port with same results. The TV is fine as it plays from my regular Blu-Ray and my Directv tuner just fine. Turned out that the TV settings for high quality sounds did not match the setup of the stick; turning that off on the TV fixed the problem.**** Another review after using for some time, two annoyances: the Home button is terribly positioned and quite often I hit it and suddenly I dont' know where I was. Why amazon, why ????? Move it away or give it a different function but not "Home" - with this, another button is missing: "Where was I" so I could return to the movie.- The rounded body of the remote makes is kinda roll on your hand... so make it square flat instead !!!-- Watchlist takes a little time to load when you turn it on;- Needs a feature to "Only show free movies" as I usually don't watch if I have to pay more.

title: Ok Alt to Roku but Not in My Living Room
body: I originally bought this device because I'd formerly subscribed to Sling but switched to PS Vue and read Vue features are different on a Fire Stick. For potential PS Vue subscribers: while fast forwarding through PS Vue recorded or live programming on a Roku, there are no screen caps; while there are screen caps while fast forwarding on a Fire Stick, they are halting and delayed, particularly when you start fast-forwarding. Secondly, my focus was so myopic that I failed to recognize there are no audio connections on the Stick. For some, that may not be a big deal, but audio-wise I could only connect the "monitor out" optical on my TV to my receiver; it was not clear if I'd have the same audio quality (DTS, etc.) as that of my Roku's direct connection (to my receiver). Additionally, I noticed the video quality with the FS was not the same as the Roku, which makes sense as the Roku is the size of my palm and has a quad-core processor, compared with the basic FS' 1.3 GHz processor. Though FS claims to broadcast at 60 fps, there was an appreciable difference between the display via the two devices--Roku was better when I viewed sports, especially, but also out-performed the FS across the board with most programs. I also did not like the Amazon-centric interface on the FS. At first glance, it was much slicker than my Roku, but a slick interface hardly matters--on Roku, I'm not moored to Amazon or Alexa at all, but can use the Amazon app alongside all my other apps (Netflix, PSVue, YouTube, etc.) without having Amazon content thrust at me all the time. (I don't use Roku's programming or interface at all.) Roku Ultra was twice the cost of the FS, however, and though sound quality is a big deal to me, I likely have a more dated audio setup than your average cord-cutter. Therefore, I'd endorse the FS, but only if you have a newer TV and are fond of Alexa control and Amazon content. I'm by and large happy with my Roku and will relegate the stick to my guest room as a cable alternative.

title: Very good device with some small issues
body: I purchased the Fire TV Stick (1080p version, I'm pretty sure) when it was on sale for almost 1/2 price. I received it a week ago and wanted to buy one for a friend and saw it was back at $40 (so no second purchase).Overall it was easy to set up, but didn't come without its problems. On the second day it lost its network connection and I had to reboot our modem/router and go through the whole setup again. I had another issue on the following day when I just shut everything down from the remote's power switch and had issues the next day, again requiring more setting up. But I've been careful to go back to home before shutting down and have had no problems since.For the price this device has many features. We have Netflix and Amazon Prime subscriptions as well as YouTube TV. The Firestick can function with all these services and has another one that we've used a lot; IMDB TV. IMDB has short commercials but seemingly very good movies. The Firestick also has apps from other services I hadn't even heard of but will have to try. One of the two annoying things is that the remote is minimal but the number of layers of screens to scroll through is a lot; perhaps too much. It is a matter of taste.The remote is small and that's good in itself. However, its uniform shape makes it difficult to know how it is oriented in your hand. Just yesterday I was trying to fast forward yet the movie went backwards... until my partner told me I was holding it upside down. I hadn't noticed. In a dark room without my reading glasses the black remote with small buttons and smaller white letters makes it difficult to know what I'm pushing. I suppose in time I'll know where stuff is from memory. If this is a potential issue for you as well perhaps you should look into one of the Roku devices.To be honest, had the Firestick been at full price I'm pretty sure I wouldn't have bought it. But at the price I paid I feel I got an incredible value.

title: Optimization System Storage and Applications
body: I usually don't review anything but I will when it doesn't work. I gave it a try for about a week. When it worked it worked great but it keeps rebooting at random times then starts a Optimization of System Storage and Applications. Got a Roku Express and we had no problems with anything. I watch the Optimization Screen more than I watch a stream. Contacted a tech and they said they fixed well it was not fixed. All of the other fixes like hold the Home button at the end of optimization or other stuff found on YouTube doesn't matter it still randomly reboots then cycles many times optimizing the system storage and applications. This was a new device I just purchased and used for less than a week. Not to mention the return policy it has to be not used. Well I returned one device and the other will serve as a reminder of a lesson learned.Edit: Amazon contacted me shortly after posting this review. Amazon is sending a replacement. I hope that this issue will be resolved.2/6/2018: Received replacement. I plugged it into where the old device was plugged in. Then again it started with the reboot again and Optimization System Storage and Applications. Upset that the replacement was doing this as well I called tech support. Finally I get this man on the phone and I found out it was the monitor I had it plugged into was not HDCP compliant. We plugged it into a TV that I had nearby and the rebooting and optimization stopped. I changed rating higher but they need to specify that it requires a HDCP compliant device.2/7/2019My HDMI switch caused the Fire TV to reboot and perform optimization.These are the culprits of my Fire TV Stick issue:Fosmon HD1831 3-Port HDMI Switch with Pigtail Cable Incompatible with Fire TV Stick because devices that require HDCP 2.2 or higher may be incompatible.ASUS VS247H-P 23.6" Full HD 1920x1080 2ms HDMI DVI VGA Monitor Not HDCP compatible.

title: Love it, mostly
body: Out of the box I was disappointed with the remote. I couldn't get it opened to insert the batteries. Finally my husband used a screwdriver and forced it open. Meanwhile I had gotten onto Chat for support and they had pretty quickly agreed to send me a replacement, which would have arrived in three or four days. So I had to get on chat support again to get them to cancel the replacement. That was quick and easy. But if you have difficulty getting the battery compartment open, just use more force, and insert a small screwdriver to get it open. It's not as delicate as it seems at first. Support was excellent.But I'm disappointed in the way the remote works. I would compare e it to a walkie talkie, not so much an Alexa device. You have to press a button in order to talk to the remote. So you have to have the remote in your hand in order to control the tv and turn it on/off.Our setup is that we have cable on just the living room tv. I got the Firestick for a second tv in a bedroom. And that second tv now is preferable to the livingroom tv. The remote makes it much easier to navigate. But the Alexa function is clumsy. I don't feel comfortable with the walkie talkie function, and Alexa isn't really smooth, though I suppose it will improve. Suggestions for use do pop up on screen. So there's a learning curve for me, and I suppose for the Alexa system. I do like the remote and the way it controls the screen content. But the Alexa interface isn't so satisfactory.I have several Alexa devices scattered around the house, so I'm familiar with the interface, and like it, and am addicted to it. But I just don't like the way Alexa works in this application. Or more accurately, I don't like the button. And in a setting where you have several people watching, only the person with the remote controls the tv, just as with an older remote system. And the remote is very small, and easily lost, so beware.

title: It’s a gamble!
body: Okay, bear with me, because I think the Amazon Fire Stick is wonderful and I like it more than I like Roku. But I have had some problems to work through.I bought my first fire stick in July of 2018, and it was the series one version. In 2019 the series two (which is the one listed here) came out and I wished that I could upgrade because I loved the changes that came with the new version (ie: volume control, can turn your tv on/off, integration with YouTube app on your phone, etc).... I bought the series two for my mom and I loved the new features but it never worked right for her. It was constantly disconnecting to the WiFi network — I tried restarting, etc, and even got a WiFi extender for her house. She could use the fire stick for short periods, but eventually it would happen again and she would get frustrated and give up. I assumed it was a problem on her end, not with the fire stick.So when my own old fire stick started going through batteries at an extreme rate (having to change every couple of weeks), I snagged a new one on sale for 20 bucks in November 2019.Low and behold - the same issues my mom had, I have too. The fire stick constantly loses connection to the network. It will play some things okay, like YouTube, but Netflix goes in and out every ten minutes or so, and disneyplus stops loading after about two minutes.Then I discovered that you can connect the fire stick to work with your iphone as the remote.So I returned the new fire stick two, and then hooked up my old series one to work with my phone.I’m sad I don’t have the cool new features, but the old stick works just fine.If you’re lucky enough to get a fire stick that works, ITS AWESOME. if you’re like me and you got unlucky, just know you are not alone in your frustrations.

title: Convenient and Easy to Use, but Software Reliability is an issue
body: Love having a single small device to control my TV, sound-bar, and streaming services. I use this with an Amazon Recast, so it controls both network streaming services like Prime Video and Netflix as well as my local stations. The voice recognition is very good, even though being a creature of habit, I routinely pull up the guide and select apps the old-fashioned way. What I don't like about the device is that there appears to be a software reliability issue, where the Fire Stick software freezes up. Cycling power on the TV has no effect. The only thing that clears the issue is to power-cycle the Fire Stick itself, except that the power button on the remote doesn't power-cycle the stick, there is neither a reset nor power button on the Stick, leaving my only options to (a) reach behind the TV (I have a large TV mounted on a wall, so this is a precarious proposition!), holding the TV with one hand while I find the USB power connection on the stick, removing and then re-inserting the USB connector; or (b) power-cycle the entire room via the circuit breaker in our house. As an embedded software engineer, I would have thought that the device would have a watchdog-reset to detect and handle a failure of this sort, but such is clearly not the case. I also would have expected that the power-button on the remote or power-down on the TV it's connected to would put the Fire Stick in a standby state and cause it to disconnect from any Amazon Recast tuner it were streaming from, but such is not the case, resulting in a curious state where, when there is only one TV powered-on in the entire house, that the Recast would report that all of its tuners are in use by other Fire Sticks, and that I must then engage in a recon mission to find which devices are actually still consuming resources of the Recast box.

title: Strange interaction with my TV.
body: This is my second firestick. The first one works great with an Amazon Fire TV Recast. So I bought a second one so that I can access the Recast on a TV in another room. Both TVs are Vizio TVs from Costco, but the second one is newer.The second Firestick on the secondTV has some strange interaction with the HDMI port on the TV. I cannot turn on the TV with the remote if the Firestick is on. I have to turn off the Firestick, and then turn off my TV, and then I can turn on my TV with the TV remote. So I have to use Firestick remote to turn OFF the Firestick and leave it off, or else I cannot turn on my TV.To use the Firestick, I have to make sure the Firestick is OFF, turn on my TV, turn ON the firestick, and then change the input selection for the TV to the Firestick HDMI. I think it has something to do with the TV's automatic detection that something active is on one of the HDMI ports.What I wanted to do is leave the Firestick ON, leave the TV input selection to the firestick HDMI, and turn on the TV and view the firestick display as the power on screen. But as it is, the firestick on the second TV is not useable. So I will have to experiment some more to see if I can work something out, but for now, the firestick is useless.Jan 31, 2020. I did some more experimenting, and it appears that the problem is that my TV does not turn on the firestick when I power on the TV with the TV remote even though the firestick's HDMI port is the selected input device. If I use the firestick remote to turn on the TV, both the firestick and TV get turned on and everything works fine. So I'm going to change my rating to 4 stars. I'm considering giving it 5 stars except for this little compatibility issue, but the firestick functionality is working just fine.

title: Better Than the One I Bought In 2014
body: I bought the original Fire TV Stick in 2014 when they first announced it. I used it on and off but we had cable and a DVR so I put it in a drawer. Recently I had some financial setbacks and canceled all the cable services. At about that time Amazon sent me an email offering a major discount on the new Fire TV Stick with Alexa integration. I bought it, delivery took a while and was even delayed beyond the promised delivery date. Installation was cake. Plug the wall wart into an outlet, plug the HDMI extender (recommended for better streaming) and USB power cable into the Stick. Mine oddly wasn't preregistered to my account but that took only a few minutes time to resolve. The longest time drain was downloading and applying the numerous firmware updates that were available. That took the better part of an hour, hour and a half or so.Once done and running everything's been fine. Any streaming quality issues I attribute to my cable company constantly needing to work on the equipment in the area. I often had problems with the cable TV quality dropping or cutting out entirely. The new Stick is a bit snappier than the old, it's got a bit faster CPU though, sadly in five years the storage hasn't been updated. It still has the same 8gb the original came with. Alexa integration is nice, though mostly good for asking for specific shows to be played or asking for the next episode to be played before waiting for the onscreen prompt. Getting the weather displayed as on a Show is a bonus.I think the best part of the whole purchase is the Stick's remote controlling the power and volume of my television keeping me from needing two remotes to watch TV. With the bargain price I'm glad of the purchase. I don't think I'd have bought it at full price but I'm glad I did when it was for sale.

title: Great, but!
body: Received my new Fire Stick today. Installation and reinstalling apps went smoothly. My previous Fire Stick was close to 4 years old which is ancient in tech time. I hadn’t noticed that the picture on this smaller, older TV wasn’t so good, but after installing the new Stick, the picture is vastly improved, sharper and clearer. The remote has features the previous remote did not have. I used to use the TV’s remote for on/off and volume control. From now on, it will be just one remote. I haven’t yet used Alexa enough to give an opinion. This Fire Stick quickly brings up what I click on, unlike my old Fire Stick which could take soooo long for some apps to get going. So far, I am very satisfied with this Fire Stick, so why 4 stars?Having had my old Fire Stick replaced at least once if not twice, I had to deal with installing batteries into the remoteseveral times as well as replacing old batteries. Amazon, you must know by now that the back of the remote does not come off the way your pictures indicate it does. I thought by now you would have redesigned this! I had go to You Tube to get instruction on how to open the back. If such a video exists, then I am not the only one frustrated by the back of the remote.
