Don’t get me wrong, I really appreciate the upgrades on the front of the remote, but why do you still make the back as frustrating as putting furniture together. You are Amazon, after all, you can afford to buy some brain power to redesign this. Solution by the way, thank you You Tube: Just like opening a pickle jar, have rubber gloves or something rubber on both hands so you can work the two parts that need to separate. Put your thumb on the indentation on the back of the remote and press and pull down. It is still not that easy but it works.

title: Easy and Simple to use, but got Charged for HBO?
body: A) Seamless or lack thereof? - Well there is single sign-on for TV subscribers which is supposed to mean "seamless," but the one thing I did not absolutely like is, I ended up being charged for a month-long HBO subscription despite it displaying the “Start 7-day trial”. Although the trial was previously "used" via the Fire-Stick, the same Prime account displayed the 7-day trial on both the Fire Stick and the iPad; so once I clicked on the 7-day trial, my Credit Card was charged. Not so seamless after all.B) Applications - The App store could have more applications with a stronger vetting process as there are times where Apps just crash. The broadcast/streaming companies putting up Apps could have a similar UX (it is really difficult to find Live TV within an App (sometimes it's on the top or bottom or the side). Pluto TV has a good Guide Like Interface and all broadcast companies should strive for a likewise interface.C) Alexa is not always on Cue – Alexa stopped working once, so I had to restart the device, Many a times Alexa will not pull up the App despite using the word “App” at the end of the App name, but instead it will try to play some Streaming Audio that sounds similar to the App name, or try to pull up a video stream of the same name within another App instead of the primary App.C) Now to the good part. What I like is the ease-of-use; overall User Interface and Experience (UI/UX). Not having more/expanded memory and a LAN port, are some of the drawbacks I found. But in such a small device I can't really expect it to do everything. I also like that there’s an option to install third-party apps at your own risk AND that’s a BIG selling point for Fire TV; without which sales would dwindle into a nose-dive really fast.

title: HONEST REVIEW
body: I had purchase this about a year ago. I originally got it to replace the TV boxes in other rooms than the main room that I didn't want to pay for with my cable subscription. I use Netflix, Disney Plus, Hulu, and now Sling TV. I haven't had any troubles with the connection or the quality of the streams on any of these. Actually, that's not all true-- when there was a really big storm-- the streaming did take a but longer and it lagged but I think that had more to do with the wifi than the stick. I enjoy using the Alexa feature because it helps me find a lot of what I don't want to look up.I bought it instead of the Roku because I thought it would be easier to manage for my technology illiterate parents and after some teaching-- they seemed to get a handle of it. There is a lot of choices on the home screen and it isn't as well organized as I had hoped but they are able to switch between their apps fine.I have Xfinity cable and as of right now (12/25/2020) this is not compatible with Xfinity Stream-- they did come out with a beta test but it only works with Smart TVs and the Roku stick, which I do not have. That is really my only complain. I don't know if because this is an "older model" that this happened but there was a time in which I had to unplug all the systems and replug and download everything back on it. Not sure why that happened but it has been working fine since.Recently, one of the remotes had broken and I couldn't use it but the customer service rep was so nice in helping me replace my stick.Overall, I have been very happy with my decision-- its simple and easy to manage all my streaming services and give me suggestions of what I should watched based on what I have watched or what new programs are available.

title: Easy to install and worth price
body: Over a year ago, I purchased a Fire TV Stick for use with the TV in our living room. It has always provided good service when turned on. About a month ago, I purchased an LG Blu Ray set which included Fire Stick capability built into its system. This player was to be used in another room in our lower level live-in basement. From day 1, the LG player would not / could not recognize the Wifi in our home. After a couple of weeks, I finally quit trying and purchased 3 new Fire TV Sticks through Amazon for use with our 3 other TVs. The first one was connected to the TV in which I had had issues with the LG player. It recognized the Wifi immediately, and connected with no issues. The other 2 were subsequently set up on other TVs and booted with no issues. Alexa works, the sticks recognize my voice, and the streaming is as if the movies are directly connected to my cable system. These are one of the best electronic purchases I have ever made,....approximately $25.00 per stick.FOLLOW-UP: One of the Firesticks that I purchased in Dec 2019 has quit working. It is connected properly, the batteries are good, and it will turn on the TV. However, Alexa no longer works, and there is no signal from the Firestick to the TV. The WIFI is not the problem since I receive it on other TVs and the Firestick programming boots up properly on those TVs. I've reduced the rating from 5 to 4. Six months of service on this product before breaking is not a good deal. If Amazon cannot resolve, then the rating will drop to a 3.2nd FOLLOW-UP: A rep from Amazon was able to resolve the Firestick issue, and my TV is now pulling in Amazon Prime and all my other apps.Thanks to Amazon.

title: Works Great So Far, A Few Minor Issues
body: I rated the Fire TV Stick as 4 stars mainly because we have only used it for about 1 month. This is our first experience with what is the equivalent of a smart TV and we are very pleased. We live in a rural area without a lot of internet provider options, so we have DSL internet service that runs at around 12Mbps. And at that speed the Fire TV Stick works just fine with fast response and loading of video streaming materials, similar to watching video material on my laptop. Some reviews I read before buying said they had trouble with the Fire TV Stick located in a cabinet, but our TV is inside an oak cabinet, and it works just fine.I will mention a few minor issues: 1) we do not have a voice activated interface with the Fire TV Stick (i.e. Alexa), so we have to use the provided alpha-numeric system along with the remote to input information, and this system is slow and cumbersome, but we are not interested in Alexa, so we make do with this system. 2) In about 5 weeks of using the Fire TV Stick we had trouble with it accessing the internet once, even though all our other devices were using the internet just fine, but we reset our router and it started working again, so no big deal. 3) We have also noticed that if we use the "Recent" icons to select a service such as Amazon Prime Music, it won't bring up the service for some reason, so we just scroll down to another icon for the service and it works. This seems odd, but it is really no problem once the Fire Stick had trained us on its minor limitations.Overall, we are very pleased with our purchase and are enjoying it daily.

title: If your Fire Stick has trouble staying connected, READ THIS!
body: Like a lot of people, i was extremely disappointed when I saw that my Fire TV stick was having trouble staying connected. It would stay connected for 20 minutes and then never fix itself, causing me to have to go into the wifi settings, forgetting my wifi network, and reconnecting, only for the same thing to happen again. Not wanting to return the thing after I just got it, I decided to look on the internetI found some answers fairly quickly, including a webpage dedicated to 10 different ways of fixing the issue. Yeah, thats right, 10. The answer i ended up going with and seemingly ended up working came from an Amazon Questions page. Heres what I did:1. Shut off my TV.2. Turned off my Fire Stick by unplugging it, then, unplugging it from my HDMI port.3. Plugged the Fire Stick into the provided HDMI extender, and plugged the HDMI Extender into the HDMI port.4. Plugged the Fire Stick back in and then turned on my tv and waited for the Fire Stick to load.I can safely say after 2 days its been working extremely well with only super brief hiccups in the stream, but that might just be due to the channel I was watching at the time. So if anyone is having trouble, this is the trick that worked for me, try it out!Everything else with the fire stick has been really awesome. Having so many options on what to watch from regular TV, to Youtube, to Twitch, all by just paying $40 is something you just cannot beat. If you can solve the possible connection issues fairly quickly like I did, you might not go back to regular cable ever again!

title: VERY EASY TO SET UP; WOULD NOT PLAY AUDIO FOR STANDARD DOLBY ON NETFLIX WITH MY THEATER RECEIVER
body: I really like how easy this is to configure, and navigation is really simple with the remote (except I need to figure out how to search Amazon prime with the remote. There are menus that pop up in my other apps-Netflix, HBO, Hulu for example). My only complaint is that for some odd reason, it won't play the audio (consistently) with my home theater receiver (Onkyo) on Netflix shows that are standard Dolby (they show as English-original). It plays any that are 5.1 fine (they show as English 5.1). No audio would play at all if Stereo was selected, and I did not have Dolby Digital Plus selected. The audio setting is set to Best Available. I was on the phone with tech support, and she just continually kept having me remove and add the receiver (which was annoyingly time consuming). I tried ALL the audio options and nothing worked, but of course once I was on the phone with them and we selected Always Dolby Digital, it played. However, after I exited Netflix and went back in to play the show, it wouldn't play audio again. I would say it played audio about 5% of the time. Amazon tried to blame it on my receiver, but it has to be a problem with the Fire Stick, as my Apple TV box and DVD player attached to the same receiver play the audio from any Netflix shows fine, regardless of the audio assigned for the show. Googling you will also find a lot of people with problems using their receivers with their Fire Sticks. Ultimately, I had to move the Fire Stick up to my 16-year-old TV so we could watch any Netflix shows with audio, and I moved the Apple TV box down to the home theater.

title: The most important: its Wifi connection coverage and strength!! This is the BEST!
body: This is a comment for everyone using Roku 4K USB stick (not the + USB stick) and suffered by low wireless connectivity strength.My Wifi router is Asus RT-AC68U , it works and covers huge area in my house (1600 sqt). I had been having issue that my Roku USB 4K stick wireless connection strength to the Wifi router. 2.4Ghz, 5Ghz , it was good a few days and then no connections and required manually reconnection again to get better strength and dropped and again and again. No matter what I did adjust my Wifi Router, it just didn't work reliably.I got Amazon FireTV USB stick for free from activation SlingTV! Good time to check if it is better with another USB stick brand. Surprisingly, the Amazon FireTV stick has greater connection signal strength and coverage from the same spot I have the old TV to my wifi router, it does reach to the Wifi router with 2.4/5Ghz GOOD SIGNAL strength. I don't even connect 5Ghz but 2.4Ghz and connected 2.4Ghz , and it gave me 12Mbps download , much better bandwidth for streaming 1080p or even 4K content. (too bad my old TV for my dad is only capable for 720p , good enough for 89y senior watching Foxnews from SlingTV - Sling TV updates now include FoxNews , I left Youtube TV and paid only Sling TV with foxnews/fox business for $35/month, fantastic!)Amazon FireTV 's Wifi chip has better signal connection range than Roku 4K USB Stick (maybe Roku upgraded better with their new Roku 4K USB + Stick but I don't bother to spend another $50 for that,,,non-sense~)Good luck!

title: Fire TV Stick Gen 1 versus Gen 2
body: I purchased my Fire TV Stick Gen 1 the day it was available at a national retailer (initials are BB) a day or two before Amazon offered it online. The coroner does not know why my Gen 1 finally died after only six years: may it rest in peace.My Gen 2 replacement arrived today (3/24/20). Opening the battery cover was very difficult. Once off and back on, it almost feels too loose. The corners of the remote need better finishing: they are a bit scratchy at the edges compared to Gen 1. The on-screen choices look different and there is a little learning curve to get used to it.On/off button: if it times out after non-use, who needs an ?Volume control: I like it; much handier than simultaneously using my TV remote and Fire Stick.Mute: useless: click the main button to stop; then you won't go back for whatever you missed.I am still glad I have Amazon Prime, but the viewing selection is a bit limited and I do not want to binge watch subscription channels on trial basis to get the series I want to watch. I can only afford one plan and will not purchase Netflix, CBS, Disney,or any of the others.Bottom line: though I can watch Prime all day on my 21" PC, I like to relax with a slightly larger screen and bought the Gen 2 replacement stick. I will end up liking it as much as the old Gen 1.Oddly enough, right now a Gen 1 replacement remote (NO STICK) is $27.99. The newer Gen 2 remote WITH A STICK is $22.99 as a Prime member ($39.99 w/o Prime). Join Prime before you buy!

title: Occasional buffering problems, but a nice unit.
body: I bought this FireTV stick as the first step in cutting the chord to DirecTV (We also finally killed our landline which was only good for spam calls!). We'd been paying for the top package for about 8 years, which crept up from the promo of $59.99 to the current $166.99 per month. While you get all the movie channels, we typically only watch original programming on HBO, Showtime, and Starz, and so we were paying a lot of money for services we don't use. Because I need DIRECTV for the NFL Sunday ticket package (they have exclusive rights, although I am hopeful the league will sell it to a cable provider during the next negotiation in a couple of years), I dropped my subscription to the 2nd lowest level (need ESPN for the Monday night games), which knocked almost $100 bucks off my monthly cost. I still get local channels as well.I paid $35 for the FireTV stick on sale, and now subscribe a la carte to HBO NOW ($15/mo.) and showtime ($12/mo.). My experience has generally been positive, but despite having gigabit internet, I'm still getting occasional buffering problems. This is unusual, as the TV it's connected to has Netflix and Amazon Prime Video apps that rarely have buffering issues. (I had to buy the FireTV stick because my TV is 7 years old, and could not be updated for the HBO/Showtime apps). One time, rebooting the firestick worked to resolve the issue, and one time I rebooted the router. I'll update this review if it continues to be an issue, but thus far am satisfied with the unit and with the cost savings.

title: Do NOT need to use the voice activation
body: First, I want to make clear that you do NOT need to use Alexa to operate this device. It comes with a remote that you can use and NEVER have to deal with that creepy thing if you don't want to.I know some people love this voice activated stuff but I'm not one of them. I prefer using a remote so I can lose it and howl in fury as I tear the place apart searching for it.I bought this as a gift and the recipient refused to use it, believing, as I did, that the ONLY way to operate this was with Alexa. Neither of us wanted Alexa on our premises so this device sat in my closet, unopened for over a year, until today. I decided to pull it out and test it. I was delighted to find out that Alexa is an OPTION that one can simply IGNORE so I did.While I have set the device up with various shows and my Amazon account, it will probably be another few days before I actually watch it because I just don't watch that much TV. But it has the Amazon music (which I listen to all of the time) and also now podcasts (that's the reason I decided to give it a try - to listen to podcasts).It was very easy to set up my other accounts. I had no issues with the set up out of the box, enabling the Wifi, finding movies and TV shows, setting up other accounts and synching them. It was very simple and very easy. No problems at all.This is great for someone who wants to "cut the cord" and give up cable and/or satellite but still enjoy a variety of movies and shows at a fraction of the cost.

title: Easy and it works (2nd Generation released in late 2019)
body: After doing some research, I chose the Fire Stick so that I could "cut" the cable cord. So far, I am very pleased with how easy it was to set up and how well it is performing. I have a 2013 vintage Samsung 37-inch LED TV. It has two HDMI ports, but only one works with the Fire Stick (the one labelled HDMI/DVI). It so happens that this port is the only one of the two that works with my Blu ray player, but that is not an issue. I was already subscribed to Netflix and this was easy to download to the Fire Stick. I added a subscription to YouTube TV to replace my cable stations (including my local stations and PBS) and that is working fine at less than half the price of my former cable subscription, with stations I could not receive before ($53 vs. $109). All of the FREE apps for Tubi, Pluto, Crackle , IMdb and more are available and easy to download. The aforementioned DO NOT require a paid subscription. Also, I have a dual-band router from Frontier for Internet service. The Fire Stick automatically selected the 5 GHz channel and again, is running very well. Based on all of this, I would highly recommend the Fire Stick to those looking to "cut" the cord. There is so much FREE content that becomes available that you may not need to subscribe to YouTube TV, Netflix or similar services. One last thing. I did nothing special to activate Alexa. It was transparent! I recently added a male-female HDMI cable so the Fire Stick and Wi-Fi extender are not hanging from the back of the TV.

title: Great Device but there is definitely a Learning Curve Especially If You are Not Tech Savy
body: It seems simple enough but there are lots of "moving parts." Part of the process was very easy like the fire stick recognizing my wireless network so i did not need to enter that data. Without any instruction it recognized the TV's and can turn them on and off as well as adjust the volume. Then there are issues like sometimes i can delete an unwanted app and sometimes i cannot. Sometimes a program plays full screen and sometimes not. Sometimes a program plays and streams fine and sometimes the system asks me to sign up for a free trial for a program i already have as part of my service. Often it is difficult to tell if the issues are me or the system. Overall i am very pleased i made the switch to the fire stick from an old Sony DVD player that also streams. Amazon will not longer be supporting that device and at first that was disappointing but the $40 fire stick was only $15 with the discount because of no longer offering support. Once i figured out more how the remote worked (and i am still discovering features), I am liking it more and more and actually plan to buy another for one of the other TV's in my home. Finally, because of this process i am also ready to drop cable and signup for a live TV streaming service. I would not have pursued that is Amazon had not "forced" me into a fire stick. Some times the "world" whacks you and often as a result of the whack things get better. That is how i see this somewhat frustrating learning process ;)

title: An absolute lifesaver!
body: I bought this item as an alternative to our lousy Roku system. My husband gifted me with a subscription to Acorn TV (because I LOVE British mysteries!) at Christmas. After the 7 day "free trial" he tried for two weeks straight to "renew" the subscription through Roku. NOTHING he did worked and it is IMPOSSIBLE to reach a living human being to get some answers or assistance. Thus my Fire Stick purchase. Set up was super easy, and when the "free trial" ended, the subscription continued (as it was supposed to) and my credit card was charged ALL without no interruption to my Acorn service. The only thing I wasn't happy about was the fact that I had a credit balance on my Amazon account & I wished it had charged the subscription against that instead of my credit card, but in comparison to all my other issues, that was a minor inconvenience. I understand that you are supposed to be able to change the billing preference under settings, but when I attempted to do so, all it would offer was for me to replace one credit card with another - the option to deduct the monthly charge from my credit balance wasn't an option. Amazon could learn from Apple in that regard. As long as I keep a credit balance on my Apple account, all my monthly subscriptions will deduct themselves from the credit balance before assessing themselves against a credit card. However, all in all, I'm THRILLED that I can continue watching my Acorn TV.

title: Ease of installation
body: I decided against tolerating the abysmal customer service attitude of Roku any longer and switch to an optional streamer. The FireStick coincidentally was offered at a discount and, coupled with reviews I read, I purchased it. It arrived just yesterday, very cleverly and ingeniously packaged by the way, though disappointed, again, to discover it's made in China. What do we have to buy that ISN'T anymore? After reading the sketchy instructions provided, and examining the components, I set about connecting it. All together, I estimate it took fewer than five minutes, start to finish, to get it configured, registered, and working. Thus far, I'm very pleased with it, though it's channel selection procedures are not as intuitive, nor as straightforward, as Roku's, nor, evidently, is the extent of choices. But that ought to improve with experience. As others have reported, Amazon is shameless in its relentless self-promotion and, frankly, it's not merely annoying, it's an assault. I chose NOT to use Alexa, as it might be convenient, it's untrustworthy, what with many reports of its "spying" activities. I prefer to push the buttons! Overall, a good choice. As a p.s., I would caution against buying Roku, though it might well be the best among the several stream devices, because, as I said, problems go unacknowledged and customer service is anything but. Amazon, in this case, the lesser of two evils.

title: CBS All Access errors, second gen stick
body: I mainly purchased the stick to start off watching shows on CBS All Access. The CSB app on this devise works with Prime. I simply had to download the CBS app to use without Prime. While watching show on All Access I started getting error messages (UVP-1001 and UVP-1011) as the next show was loading and could not watch the show on my tv. I did try two other options. I was able to watch the next episode on my computer and Xbox. Then I could go back and watch some more on my Stick. But the error would come up again between episodes. I also unplugged the Stick once and then was able to watch an episode that previously showed an error message.So this leaves me to believe that the problem is with the Stick while viewing All Access via CBS's app and not the app that is pre installed to work with Prime only. Having to unplug the Stick all the time or gathering around the laptop is not a good option.Other than that the Stick has worked well. I do have it connected with the available Ethernet adapter on DSL. Usually just a few seconds to buffer and shows and movies start quickly.I tried the Alexa and it made me feel like I was Mr. Scott sitting at a computer talking into a mouse.And for those that want free movies and shows, there are apps that can be installed that state free TV. One already installed is IMDB TV. Free shows and movies with commercials.

title: Doesn’t last long.
body: I’ve had many fire sticks since they have came out with them. I love it for the most part because I have everything that I could possibly want to watch right there. I can move from my bedroom to the living room and pick up right where I left off on the same movie. I bought them for my parents and they love it too. But the flaw is that I’ve never had a fire stick last for more then a year/ year and a half without starting to have problems. My first fire stick which was one of the original ones worked great and then it started to get slower and slower, the remote stoped working. So I had to use my phone as a remote on the app and then my phone wouldn’t connect so I had to use my Alexa dot to control it. To replace the remote it cost practically as much as a new fire stick. It was out of warranty so I couldn’t have it replaced and the new fire stick came out so I just thought oh I will buy a new one and I did. I love it a lot but now getting my Alexa to control it is very difficult it took me three/ times to get it to connect properly.While these are all minor things it’s just something to think about when buying these. They are relatively inexpensive and amazon usually does really good deals around the holidays to get them cheaper. But I just wish they lasted a little bit longer so I don’t have to keep replacing them all the time.

title: Compatability issues update
body: I was very excited about the opportunity to make my older HD television more functional and not have to buy a new smart TV. After enjoying my Fire Stick for a week my television began to malfunction. It started to automatically switch on and off in random short cycles. I thought my old TV had just wore out because the symptoms were similar to when capacitors go out. Well before just tossing it out, I had to experiment. After a few hours of trying different things I discovered that did I not only have to remove the Fire Stick, but had to take it to an entirely different room of the house. This just happened last night so I have much more research to do and am hopeful the unit will work on a different TV in my house, but since I was prompted for a review just now, well, just bad timing I guess.Update:Having not identified the exact cause of my problem I decided to recreate the original setup and do some more checking. I have not been able to locate the problem so far because over nearly one month I have had no problems whatsoever. Sorry, I can't tell you what the the problem was. Maybe I just wasn'tholding my mouth right. I don't know. But, now that it is working, I can report that the Fire Stick is an amazing piece of technology and, with the savings on my entertainment bill, is probably one of the best investments I have made.

title: my 1st hd stick
body: so i have a 4k stick and it's wonderful. this one was ordered for an older smart panasonic 1080p set and is hd (1080) only. panny has been horrific when it comes to support for its smart tv updates, so i had to buy the stick. lg is is better but they need to do more.overall, i'm very pleased. it's a bit ezier to set up when compared to their 4k stick but both are essentially no-muss, no fuss.what i really like about this stick is the plethora of apps, including a vpn, over and above roku+. we don't have youtube tv yet but i'm hopeful. hulu could use some additional comp.so here's the negative stuff. if u like hulu with live, plan on having to reboot the stick with some regularity. why? there are times when the app it just hangs after the 2nd button press within the hulu app. a reboot will allow one to get directly into hulu forthwith, but subsequent launches result in the hulu profile page displayed first. amazon and hulu need to figure this out.in fairness, i've not experienced this behavior with this hd (not 4k) stick but i rarely use it and suspect that the issue is os based and likely pervasive with multiple versions of the stick.so that's it though! and yes, i can live with that flaw. overall, connectivity is good and for the $$, it's a better choice for me over the roku+

title: Better than expected
body: I am pleasantly surprised that this works so well. I had been looking at the Roku streaming stick+ but it went out of stock, and this was only 30$ instead of $50, so I figured I'd give it a shot.I'm glad I did. Setup was easy, though I was surprised it did not come already linked to my Amazon account like all my other amazon devices have been. It was not a hassle to log in to link them, but it was a step that I didn't expect to take.Logging in to each service was painless, and the downloading of the apps for each non-prime service was quick. I am able to use my 5G wifi band for this, which is a huge bonus.Picture and sound quality are fantastic and I love that the remote takes over my TV, so I don't need to use 2 remotes to operate it.I do wish alexa was better integrated. I don't like having to hold the button down on the remote to speak, as that negates hands-free usefulness. I have an Echo Dot right by our TV and it'd be nice to be able to use that instead of the remote, or to be able to push the remote button once to activate voice, then be able to put it down. It's usually easier for me just to navigate with the remote than try voice commands.Overall though I am very happy and this was better than I expected it to be. Would buy again.

title: Solid Streaming Device
body: I now own three of the top streaming devices out there: Apple TV, Roku, and now Fire Stick.The Apple TV is the most robust of the three I'd say in terms of apps and games, and how fast it is and amount of storage. I'd say the Roku would come in second in terms of how snappy it is. Honesty all the devices have all the apps that I could possibly want, so I can't ding any of them for that.What I will say about the Fire Stick is that it's probably the slowest of the three. It's not painfully slow, just not as snappy as my Apple TV or Roku. That being said, it integrates really nicely with Alexa if you have a smart home and are in the Alexa ecosystem, and the stream quality is solid. Also, out of the three devices, this was the cheapest, and goes on sale frequently for like $29, which is a steal. It's not the fastest or most robust streaming device money can buy, but it's also affordable and gets the job done.I feel like I've kind of knocked on it a bit in this review, but really I'm quite satisfied with it considering the price. There are more expensive Fire TV devices like the Cube which has a faster processor and such so I'm sure is much snappier. All that said, when I need another streaming device this will probably be the one I'll buy. Certainly worthy of 4*'s.

title: Randomly turns off and gets hot **UPDATE 8/6**
body: (First one purchased on 5/21/19.) Set up was a breeze, it synced with my TV immediately so the volume buttons worked great. 1 day later, it randomly shut off while watching Netflix. I unplugged it and then it came back on for 2 mins and shut off again. I thought it felt sort of warm, but it was odd as I had only been watching TV for about an hour. I switched to cable for a bit and when I went to turn it on again NOTHING. It would not turn back on. I tried everything from batteries in the remote, switching plugs on the wall and HDMI ports on the TV, even button combinations to manually reset the fire stick. Nothing.I finally unplugged it from the extender, then it came on! But the quality was SOO poor it buffered every 30 seconds, so it still didn't work properly. I have started the return process and plan to order another one once I have my money refunded.I have the 1st generation fire stick and it is doing great! *Knock on wood* I wanted a 2nd stick for the bedroom, so hopefully 2nd time is a charm for this one and I can update this review.**UPDATE! 8/6/2019**2nd firestick works as described! I've had it for about 2 weeks with No issues! Very happy with the new one. The first one I ordered must have been defective, but amazon was quick with the return process.

title: Does not work with Alexa dot already in the room.
body: I set this up for me and my parents while I am here taking care of them. I think it is too difficult for my 87 yo parents to operate. Main thing is it does not seem to work with the Alexa dot already in the room. You must use the controller. When I turn on using the controller the tv turns on but stays with the HDMI1 which is the cable input (box is off) so I must have the original TV controller to select the source.I have set up Youtube to look at exercise videos but there is no convenient way to save them to watch later. I have to create lists on the computer before I can use them on the screen.When I have reviewed movies and select one it often tells me I have to have a subscription to another service. I do not want to pay for any more services but there does not seem to be an easy way to set up preferences not to see them.I know this is easy for some but I do not have time to explore. I rely on my innate abilities with computer and this system is not intuitive.The video quality is good and i have not had any problems with loading videos. I also like the watch list so I can pre-select movies for us to watch. I have not figured out how to remove a Thursday night football game I put on my watch list then watched at a friends. I just get an error.

title: Nice little streamer.
body: I had some courtesy credits from amazon for late deliveries and the only way to redeem them was to purchase an amazon product fulfilled by amazon.This was the closest thing I could find for something I thought I might use. I already have a roku tv and streaming through tivo, but thought this would be handy for taking on vacation since it is so portable, and I really like the remote.After trying it out this weekend, it is a nice little streamer and I like how if you ask alexa something like the weather or a game (I like song quiz) it will put info. And graphics on the tv to see while playing.I also like how I was able to set up the remote to change the volume and channels for the tv and reciever.I thought it was cool to have my bluetooth headphones connect to the firestick, until I realized that somehow it sucks away some of the wi fi bandwidth, and I lost clarity with the picture. There was a warning about this when I tried it. I could have purchased the optional cable to hook directly to the firestick, but I read too many reviews that said that didn't always help. Plus, they should have thrown it in anyway.It's a decent streamer with some nice features, but nothing that really stands head and shoulders above the others in its class and price.

title: If you are streaming Live Tv get the 4 k instead
body: I own both the FireStick and the FireTV Stick 4k. Got them both last year in 2019. I use them frequently. I am in a 2 week trial of YouTube TV as I am moving away from satellite. The FireStick only receives live tv up to 720p, the 4k stick receives live tv @ 1080p. I also receive much higher bandwidth with the 4k, in fact sometimes with the FireStick it defaults down to 480 or 360. Even with the fire stick in the TV closest to the modem/router ( about 4 foot away) the Fire Stick still only receives in 720p. I recently found this out because the app, YouTubeTv, has a selection where you see what resolution you are getting. I have DSL and at best where I live I can get up to 12,000. So I cannot get 4k. On my 65 inch LG OLED TV Amazon Prime app I can get sometimes high rez and HDR, but usually it defaults to HD ( 1080 ) and/or HDR. My other TV's are older LG 55 inch 3d 240 3d TVs. They are 1080p max. However when I stream in archived files from Netfix or Amazon Prime, or on demand shows off of YouTubeTv, like The Late Show, Colbert (CBS) it moves up to 1080. For live streams, even if you have a 1080 HD set, GO with the FIreStick TV 4 K, its got 2 gigs of RAM and 8 gigs of storage and a super quadcore processor, and, the picture and sound quality are great.

title: Volume control? Not MY job, says Firestick..
body: The Firestick (vs. Chromcast...)Good: Amazon videos stream as well as anyone else's videos. The control is more a menu system than Chrome's background source-app style. This makes the FIrestick more of a platform than "just an interface", and you can add apps to the Firestick. Streaming is nearly flawless. The controller provided works well. Setup was easy.Bad: Firestick relies on the HDMI end device to control sound volume (where Chromcast uses HDMI volume). Two issues with this: IF you use an HDMI sound extractor like I do, there's no volume control from the Firestick remote. And secondly, if your TV or HDMI display isn't on the really-long-list of possible devices, good luck finding the right sound volume controls for your device. By contrast, Chromecast allows intuitive volume control from your phone - just use the volume up & down on the phone!Also bad: the Android phone app for Firestick doesn't function worth a hill of beans on my Galaxy J7 with Android 8.0.0. I fear loosing the Firestick remote... Again, Chromecast is fairly well integrated in the phone, and Google Home makes a good safety net when a particular app fails to control the Chromecast (as sometimes happens).

title: Pretty cool! - Not fully compatible with 10yr old TV, but still good!
body: Got this during prime day for $15. Can't beat that! Put it on my 10 yr old "non smart" Sharp TV. The Fire stick works well with the various apps, but is not compatible with all my tv's functions.According to the directions, the Fire Stick remote and Alexa should be able to switch inputs from the "HDMI/Fire Stick" input to the regular tv or cable input (and back to the HDMI/Fire Stick input). I should also be able to change regular tv channels using this remote. None of that works with the Fire stick and my TV. I tried various settings. If I attempt to do any of the above, it seems to hang my tv for a bit and I can't even use my TVs regular remote for a few minutes. I have to manually turn off my tv for the Fire Stick remote and my tv's original remote to work again.So... It works great while using apps, but it's not compatible with my other TV functions that it should also be able to control. I'm ok with that. I just keep both remotes. I use the Fire Stick remote for all the smart TV functions and apps, and when I want to go back to watching regular tv, I use my TV's original remote.I'm still very happy with this purchase. I'll probably get more when they're on sale again.

title: Better than before - but still needs tweaks!
body: I will absolutely say the FireTVstick is an upgrade from the FireTV. Setup was easy, and I really like the HDMI extension for those of us who have their TV mounted and can’t just have the FireTVstick, sticking out in the back of the TV.The new remote is awesome since it includes the volume buttons, which the older versions lacked and it also comes with batteries for the remote (Amazon Basics) which is also a huge plus!That said, the issues I have are that the NBC (or any affiliate app, like Bravo) doesn’t like to work all the time - it works properly about 65% of the time. It freezes often, buffers often, loses connection often and it’s not the WIFI because the FireTV that we have in the next room works perfectly, all the time.The last issue I have is not necessarily specific to the FireTVstick but I really don’t like that the RedBox on Demand app is not available!!! This would be a great addition for those of us who cut the cord and rely on streaming services/devices to watch TV/movies.I assume the two issues I have noted could easily be fixed by Amazon pushing an update to the FireTVstick.Did you find this review helpful? Let me know!

title: regular intermittent audio dropouts which seem to be fixed temporarily by power cycling
body: Had it only 2 days. I'm only using it for the amazon music app. Since installation, the music drops out for 1 second once or twice each song, then it restarts where it left off. WiFi strength is reported as very good. This replaced a Roku stick in this tv that never, ever had any audio issue...even when using the roku's amazon music app. Gotta be a firestick issue.FYI: The trick to removing the back cover is to use something rubber or rubber-like that is able to "grab" the plastic while you pull it down. This should be included in the packaging. Or the back needs a rubbery coating in future versions.Update: after a restart (under the settings menu) it seems to now be behaving itself.2nd update: restarting it only helps for a couple hours before the audio stalling returns.3rd update: many days later & nothing has changed.4th update: I've noticed that the longer it plays between restarts the longer the pauses get.5th update: after almost 30 days trying to solve this issue I'm throwing in the towel & returning it.6th update: bought the 4K firestick thinking it might not have the issue. It does.

title: GREAT DEVICE BUT PRIME SOFTWARE GETTING WORSE
body: I've been using Amazon FireStick and Amazon Prime video exclusively for TV viewing for almost 9 years. I don't even have cable, and I don't miss it a bit. In all that time, I only replaced my FireStick once, and that was because I stupidly lost the remote, and it arrived in about 4 hours thanks to Amazon Prime Now. It is high quality and very easy to use. But like most tech companies, Amazon keeps producing "upgrades" to the Prime Video channel software that sacrifice basic functions for bells and whistles. Movies are no longer included on the RECENTS list, and since I view documentaries I use to teach in my college classrooms, I do sometimes need to view a film more than once to ensure my notes are correct. Also, if I fell asleep watching a movie, it used to be easy to find it to watch again. Sometimes, even a TV series I'm watching disappears from the RECENTS list, even though it was there the day before. As with most "upgrades," each version gets a little bit worse in terms of basic functionality. I never upgrade software when I have a choice, but we're not given a choice in this case. Tech companies need to learn the expression "if it ain't broke, don't 'fix' it."

title: Great! Even for a little casual gaming.
body: First of all, I bought this mainly for the kids to play and watch kids movies after their xbox (360) died awhile back. It is the second Gen Fire TV stick I believe without the 4k. They were happy with crossy road and other little games and movies. I wanted to see what this little thing could do, so I tested Real Racing 3 from EA and Street Fighter IV from Capcom. I was pleasantly surprised how well it ran, not great but not terribly bad either. Framerate can be an issue from time to time and sound is the same way. I used a fire tv game controller which doesn't appear to be sold anymore. Probably because reports indicate they don't work with the newer gen fire tv's. Of course, there are other bluetooth controllers available as well that may work for those setups. Also note, I ran out of space pretty quick so yeah, don't recommend too much gaming and apps on your kids TV stick, or yours, as storage is very minimal, 8GB and about half is used by the system already. Of course, Fire TV stick is a streaming device first but the potential for some gaming is there. In the end, went back to kiddie movies and crossy road for the kids and they're still happy.

title: AMZN Firestick, an excellent product let down by unreliable hardware
body: I just ordered and installed the new firestick with the television controls. It’s a nice upgrade from the older model without those. This is my 6th firestick. I keep ordering them because their software is elegant, and the service is so good. They take all the intelligence needs out of your TV and put it in the multiprocessor brain that’s the guts of the firestick. Here’s the downside: the hardware is flakey and starts to fail at about a year’s age. You’ll be doing something and then bang, the system reboots. Hey, it’s a computer and rebooting is part of the game. No, when the firestick reboots it will keep on rebooting and eventually, you’ll see it’s just not going to work. By accident I found a kind of work around. And that is to age the system, unplugged, for a day or more. Then when you put power to it again it boots up nicely. The problem is that all of my firesticks, in multiple dwellings in 3 different states have exhibited the same problem. Since all of my firesticks have this problem, it’s likely that all of most of the ones AMZN has sold have this problem. So, I’ve just resigned myself to the fact that I have to replace them every other year or so.

title: The new remote is great. Controls TV and Cable box with voice. (Volume, channels, power...)
body: I ordered this on Prime Day on accident. I mean to buy the 4K stick since I already had the regular one. But the upgraded remote is nice. I just wish I would’ve paid more attention when checking out since now if I want to get the 4K version I have to pay $50 instead of $25.. :( That’s my fault though. I don’t think my older remote would control my tv but this new one will turn it on and off, control volume and even control my cable box by voice (change channels by saying “Alexa tune to channel xxxx” or “tune to ESPN”. I’m trying to figure out how to get my Echo Dot to work with it but no luck with that yet. I wish Amazon would accept a return to exchange it for a 4K stick for the discounted price but since it was my fault and not theirs, I wouldn’t actually expect them to. Lol. I’m just going to give my other one to my parents and get the one I want some other time. I really like the remote and the voice control capabilities. There are some problems that need worked out but those updates will come with time. I enjoy discovering new tasks that can be performed through voice. I’m sure there’s way more for me to learn about what I can do with the new ir remote.

title: Awesome upgrade from the original fire stick
body: I have the original fire stick and I love it. I have 2 TVs, 1 in my living room and 1 in my bedroom. Because I love the original fire stick (gift from my son ❤) I finally decided to get a 2nd for my bedroom. I bought this upgraded fire stick on Prime Day. I got it yesterday. I'm amazed that the remote can link to my TV and control the TV. I read from other reviewers that it powers on their TV. If this is true, then I will be ecstatic. My TV remote is HUGE and I hate it. The TV was built in 2006 so the TV and remote are huge. Both work great. I was originally going to just use my Chromecast as I love the casting from room to room without losing my place. However, since the fire stick remote controls the TV I am rethinking my thoughts. I was amazed that the fire stick was soooooo easy to set up. It recognized my Wi-Fi ID and my password for the WI-FI. I did not need to give the code. I think that it picked up all the information that the original fire stick has, including Wi-Fi information. This makes the upgraded fire stick the easiest set up ever. I also noticed that the speed of the processor is incredible. Not once have I noticed any buffering. I do get buffering from my other 3 devices. Guess it's time to replace my original fire stick with this one. This fire stick replaced my Roku which I've had for years and still works fine. The reason why I replace the Roku was that Roku seems to be incompatible with Hulu Live (love it BTW). It keeps shutting off the program that i am watching making me yo go back into my Hulu account again. Just imagine, you're watching a show and just at the most interesting part, it flicks and goes back to the home screen. Ahhhhh!!!! Since this fire stick performs so well, I'm thinking of not even using my 2 Chromecasts anymore. Now that's progress! The 2 Chromecasts do have some lagging and that's why I'm thinking of just using the fire stick and buying a new one for the living room. If anyone was on the fence about buying this fire stick, don't be. This purchase was well worth the money. Go for it!! I was going to upgrade my internet because I thought the lagging was them, nope. This fire stick has out performed my wildest dreams. Go buy this, fire stick you won't be sorry!! Oh one last thing, I've noticed that some reviewers have complained about opening up the battery case. It's actually easy, you simply press dojwn on the circle at the top and push the cover to the opposite side. You will hear a little pop. That's releasing the lock. Then you will see a small space where the circle is, just stick your fingernail (presuming your nail is not so bitten to the point where it's below the nail line) and push. Easy peasy! Amazon clearly have their products hooked up! I love the ease of Amazon, hands down. ❤. If my opinion changes about the upgraded fire stick, I'll post an update.UPDATE: Its very easy to navigate the fire stick but if you can't find something all you have to do is ask Alexa and she will find it for you. It also fully takes charge of your TV. There is nothing you need With the original clunky remote. Unless you want to access your HDMI ports. But with all that the fire stick offers, you don't need another dongle. I will be using the fire stick only. It's so wonderful to use one small, comprehensive remote. Basically Amazon has updated the fire stick to make it a universal remote. Now I'm going to buy a remote cover so I can easily see my new "universal" remote in the dark. ❤😎I purchased a second Fire Stick from a big box store. I'm sorry that I did. When you buy a Fire Stick through Amazon, they load it with your account information making set up super easy. When you buy one through the box box stores they are generic and set up was a bit more difficult. Suggestion folks but your Fire Stick through Amazon and forget the big box stores! Wish I would have known this before I purchased my second one! Never again!!

title: What Can I Say... I LOVE my Amazon Devices
body: This replaces my Fire Pendant and I am so happy with this new device and it's performance.1. Super easy to setup and my soundbar was instantly connected. Clear, sharp picture and sound. Remember when things are loading for the first time, it might run a little slow, but once done you should be running just fine.2. So pleased that now I can control volume and power on and off the tv. You do not need to do anything to make this happen, the stick does the work.3. Alexa feature is so great to not have to look through pages and pages of "stuff" to find what you want. If new to Alexa, just press the mic button and ask Alexa what is our weather today or how much does an elephant weigh. So simple, now ask Alexa: Play Masterpiece Theater and she will display results for you to choose from. Now some of the add on Apps do not support Alexa yet, but many do. Alexa can do a lot of things. Play with it, you are not going to break her. HINT: If I am looking for a specific show and no results with Alexa, I just simply go on line and search...Where can I watch The Untouchables and you can get results on the web.4. ***** This stick WILL NOT WORK if you have your modem set to 5GHz. You need to lower that number. Call your provider for help or search the internet for how to do. Your Home screen will come up on the TV, but the device is not going to work properly. If you try and play something and it just buffers endlessly check out the modem speed. If you select to download an app and it just will not load is another sign the 5GHz needs to be changed to a lower number. Does not affect my screen quality at all at 2.4 . ( I had this problem when I setup my stick and then remembered seeing a post about this which sure helped me.)5. Many are confused about what you can watch on the Fire Stick. Think of the stick as a doorway into a big reception/lobby. Sign over long endless hallway marked FREE CHANNELS and USE... go down that hall and find the APP you want and install. Most of these are not ad free. Another long endless hallway is marked PREMIUM CHANNELS and these are the paid subscription channels/apps. Find what you want... load the APP and then register with the channel (Netflix, Hule, Vudu, ect). Most of them just simply sign you up with your Amazon account and the monthly charge is automatically is done through Amazon. For some, you will have to go to a website that will be on your tv screen and enter a code so the company (Netflix, ex. ) recognizes your tv or device.5. Be sure and try out Amazon's IMBd for free movies, tv shows and more. The ads are fast and do not bother me at all. Great free content.6. If you receive a Fire Device and it is not working properly ... Call customer support first before posting a scathing review. They will make it right. Not fair or helpful when people post negative things about the product when not taking to time to find a possible solution. Many problems can be solved simply by searching the web for an answer.7. I was not paid nor received anything from Amazon to post this. Just reading through posts and problems people are having and the fix is usually so simple.So many said they had not tried Alexa, when it is just the simplest thing ever to do to push the mic button and ask a question or give a command. Think Suri intelligence. Makes finding what you want so much easier. Ask her to play rock music or your fav music or My Music (from your Amazon account) and BOOM the music is playing.This stick is so worth the money.

title: A Successful Experiment
body: I bought a Fire TV Stick in a pre-Prime Day sale for $15. I wasn't really sure if I wanted or needed it but, at that price, I decided to experiment. Up until now, I have been streaming using the apps on my Tivo (which I still love). Lately however, it has been acting up...breaking the streaming approximately every 6hr with error messages suggesting inadequate internet bandwidth. I subscribe at the low end of bandwidth available (~20Mb/sec) so that's a real possibility but after trying different things (mostly rebooting everything I own) I was starting to suspect the Tivo and wondered if an alternative might work better.The Fire TV Stick was chosen based on decent Consumer Reports ratings as well as user ratings with the low sale price pushing me over the edge. I also like the compact size which will readily allow me to remove it and take it with me when I travel. I chose the basic (though most current) model rather than the 4K because I still have a 1080p TV and don't intend to update it any time soon. (I have an old plasma TV that looks so good that I don't plan on changing it until it dies...or bigger OLED TVs get cheap enough that I can't stand it.)Anyway, back to the Fire TV Stick. In short, it is very nearly perfect. Installation was very easy - in my case, I had to use the included "extender" that pushes the Fire TV Stick away from crowded HDMI junctions on the back of my TV. Use is also easy though I tend to just use the controller directly as opposed to voice control. I bought my first Alexa at the same time and while I like it, I'm not entirely used to it yet. What voice commands I have tried with the Fire TV Stick have worked fine.Actual streaming has been very good. Overall, I've probably streamed over 30 hours at this point without a single interruption. The picture quality is very good...but interesting. I believe that I am streaming at 1080p and believe that my Tivo was streaming at 1080i (sometimes 720p). I'm one of the people who notice the difference between 1080i and 1080p so very much like the increased level of resolution that I am seeing but have noticed a kind of grainy quality to the picture that I've never noticed before. It's not bad or off-putting but different and interesting. I've pretty much been binging one show though so it's possible that it is a characteristic of the original that is being streamed.The only thing that I don't like as well as I'd like to is the menu structure which is similar to but not identical to what would come up on my Tivo. Neither is perfect...both are perfectly manageable.One other thing I've noticed by accident and really appreciate. I have a tendency to multi-task (e.g., reading FaceBook or the newspaper) while I watch TV...like 90% of other people. Every once in a while, I'll get so engrossed in what I'm reading that I pause the stream so I don't miss anything. After a couple of minutes, the Fire TV Stick automatically goes into a screen saver mode flashing up gorgeous pictures from around the world. A cool thing that I haven't tried (but will today) is that you can apparently load some of your own pictures into Amazon photos (I know, giving up even more of your privacy) and use your own photos for the screen saver. You don't have to but it's kind of a nice option to have.Anyway, for what I was viewing as a potentially throw-away experiment, I am extremely pleased and highly recommend this.

title: Had to make a compact ethernet installation on our 18" kitchen TV
body: I ordered this --- Fire TV stick streaming media player, with Alexa Voice Remote (2nd Gen, released 2019) --- to add ethernet streaming to an old 19" LCD TV that has coax, component, composite, VGA and 2 HDMI inputs. This TV lives in the kitchen and is attached to via coax, and sits upon, an older cable TV box. Space is tight where the TV and cable box sit at the end of the counter. I wanted an installation as compact as possible, and, use ethernet. I didn't like the looks of the VERY short Amazon brand ethernet adapter for the Fire stick, and, since it has to be purchased separately anyway, I ordered this one at the same time, --- Price: $13.89 --- UGREEN Ethernet Adapter for Fire TV Stick (2nd GEN), All-New Fire TV (2017). It has a 3.3ft USB power cable & micro USB cable. I used the USB power supply that shipped with the Fire stick. I couldn`t have the Fire stick hanging out of one of the two HDMI ports on the TV, so I included one each of the following M x F ( 2 pcs ) extension/adapter cable sets, with my order ; * Price: $9.99 -- HDMI 2.0 Extension Cable, Cable Creation [2PCS] 1.5 Feet Upward Angle HDMI 2.0 Male to Female Cable + 1.5 Feet Downward Angle HDMI 2.0 Male to Female Cable, 4K Ultra HD, Black. ---- And ---- * HDMI 2.0 Extension Cable, Cable Creation [2PCS] 1.5 Feet Right Angle HDMI 2.0 Male to Female Cable + 1.5 Feet Left Angle HDMI 2.0 Male to Female Cable, 4K Ultra HD, Black. I figured one of these four ext./ adapter cables would give me the correct direction from the side or rear HDMI port to remote mount the Fire stick. The male end of the angled ext./ adapter cable is plugged into the side HDMI port, goes up the side and over the top. The 18" length was ideal for a Fire stick location on top of the TV, the Fire stick male end is plugged into the female end of the ext./ adapter cable. I attached the cables and the Fire stick to the top of the TV with some homemade twist ties, fished through the heat vent slots. I plugged the Cat5e cable into the modem and the other end into the Ugreen ethernet adapter, chose HDMI-2 on the TV input and plugged in the USB power supply. After the yellow and green lights came on at the Cat5e end of the ethernet adapter the Fire stick powered up to the setup screen. The setup was straight forward and the Fire stick works very quickly, probably because of the ethernet connection. We have an early Fire TV box ( the flat one ), attached to our big TV via ethernet and it works quickly also. We subscribe to Amazon Prime and I enjoy listening to Amazon Music through a Creative sound card in the PC, then into a good mini amp, a pair of nice small speakers, along with a sub. This old kitchen TV has very poor speakers. It has a coaxial digital audio out connector that I hooked into a DAC then into an old pair of powered PC speakers, sound was much better, but I`d need to find a place in the kitchen for more equipment !

title: Delivered exactly as requested! These are a FANTASTIC addition to a home!
body: Bought two of these on the Prime Day Sale. Delivered a day earlyer than predicted, and placed under the gas grill on my porch exactly as requested, and they posted a picture of the delivered package on the tracking page so I would know exactly where to find it! Items themselves... In a word, Fantastic! Whether you are cutting the cable, or just augmenting your system, they are a great. Much faster than the apps on my smart blue-ray, and a great addition to my two Hi-Def dumb TV's. The only negative I have found so far, is that I had heard that if ordered from Amazon thru Prime, they would come already set up to my account -- tho' I was unsure just how they would manage that, and since neither package seemed to have been opened I was not too surprized when at least the first one I installed on my bedroom TV had to be logged into my account, but befor I was through setting up the initial unit, it was already identifying itself as my second Firestick and somehow knew I had two Firestick units! I hadn't even opened the other one yet... (So, it did know something somehow...) Even stranger since the one I later installed on my main TV also had to be logged into my account, but it had somehow communicated to the one in my bedroom, because it ID'd itself as my Firestick, and the one I installed first had id'd itself as my 2nd Firestick, even though it was the first one installed.None of which has anything to do with the product of course, but if you have read this far, we can get down to it. It works, it is fast, with no lag I can tell. As others have noted, the remote is very small, and practically impossible to open with bare hands, but very easy to open if you tack a small flat head or knife and put it in the thin junction at the top back and give it a little twist and it slides off, harly any pressure needed, could do it with a thumbnail if you have tough ones, either way you don't need enough force to scratch the remote. I had to use a knife but both came off easily after that, but no force or guile I could summon would get the thing open with out something to get it started! All of the apps work, and they are a mix of for pay subscription sites, and pure free stuff. Quite a lot of free also, but since I am a prime member already I think it will be my main base so to speak, as I can get subsciptions for HBO and others like them Cinemax, etc through Prime as I want to keep most of the for pay options in one place. Bottom line, even at the regular price this is a good buy, though if it had not been on sale I wouls have gone for the 4K version at $10 more, so I would be ready when I get a 4K TV, but since that might be a while, these turn my 1080i TVs into smart tvs at a dirt cheap price, and also comes with the Alexa functions. Since all I have is the TVs for that, I am uncertain if the Alexa remote can also drive other things like lights and other smart home products beside the TV but if so, then that will be a bonus down the road.

title: A Simple, Affordable Way to Broaden Your Streaming Media World
body: I must confess that despite having bought my Amazon Fire TV Stick quite some time ago, it took our current health pandemic for me to really give it a chance.I'm hooked.A couple months into the pandemic, my computer died and I went a few days without it until a repair could happen. Thanks to the Amazon Fire TV Stick with Alexa Built-In and Alexa Voice Remote, I barely missed a beat in terms of my daily life that includes computer work and reviewing movies.I'm an Amazon Prime member and this makes the Fire TV Stick an even better deal, though you may find yourself wanting to expand your smart world more quickly than you can actually afford with all the options the Fire TV Stick actually provides you.As someone who's not a great techie, I was amazed how easily the Fire TV Stick could be set up. While I initially had a friend come over to help, I quickly caught on and was able to do nearly everything myself, while my follow-up needs have been quickly addressed because it's so simple to use.You can easily hook it into your wi-fi, of course. That seems like it's one of those things that will be intimidating, but it's super easy and really makes everything run smoothly.You have options like Prime Video, Hulu, Netflix, Sling, ESPN, Youtube, HBO, Disney+, Tubi, and more. Many of the channels have extensive free collections, while I'd say most of them offer free trials. Of course, given the ease of use you want to make sure you don't accidentally click on subscribing if that's not your intent as certain channels are definitely subscription channels.You also have a variety of smart options that allow you to tap into your home environment, while I was also amazed how easily you could use your e-mail and browse the internet. Once you get used to the Alexa voice features, it's even easier as the keyboard feature is definitely a bit cumbersome.You can buy accessories with it that will improve your experience, though so far I've not gone that far. Truthfully, I haven't needed to improve the experience because it's a great experience.Now that I've really started using the Amazon Fire TV Stick, I'm definitely hooked and it's really added quality to my quarantine experience. It's an affordable tool that gives you so much access to both free and paid viewing experiences that I can't imagine doing quarantine without it. There are also quite a few options for those with kids and if you really dig into the different indie channels you can find everything from home improvement to self-improvement to a variety of church channels. It's all really awesome.

title: Greatest thing since Betty White
body: I have an older smart TV and Hulu doesn't work that great on it for some reason and there was no integration to add apps to the Smart functions so when Disney+ came out and Verizon gave me a free year of the service I figured it was time to get a new TV. Well my wife thought we should just get a fire stick instead because it's $35 instead of $400 for a new TV. I even managed to get a bundle with a 3rd Gen Echo Dot for all of $7 more so why not?A couple of magical things happened here. The new Echo dot was somehow able to force the rest of my Echos to work again. They had quit working nearly a year ago and after setting up the new one I gave the old ones another shot and *poof* they woke up and worked as if they knew they were about to be binned by this new replacement. Pretty sure there actually isn't any correlation here but I choose to believe! Also the television can somehow get a strong enough WiFi signal to the Firestick to play Hulu no problem at all magically despite the TV itself being on the exact same network and having issues with that app. The final bonus though was that Plex has an app on Fire Stick so now I can take my entire media server and simply stream it through the Fire Stick instead of having to use a Raspberry Pi as a "media center PC" on my home television. Bonus points because that means I can now pull my Pi off the TV and go back to doing dumb experiments with it instead of having it run as a dedicated VLC player.The Alexa voice remote is a nice touch. I like that you can talk to it, and somehow despite being within range of my other echo devices they know which one I'm talking to which is pretty cool. You can turn off the television using voice command to the remote which is pretty neat, and you can mess with your pets by whispering simon says "pet name come here" to the remote from another room and the Fire Stick will repeat your command from the TV which really screws with them. I mean who doesn't want to confuse their fluffy puppy dog because it's amusing? That's some quality entertainment value added there since I don't have to speak loud enough for my pet to know it's me which is something you can't do with a basic echo or echo dot. Next level stuff here guys. I wonder if I can drop in on one of my echos from my TV remote... Look the possibilities of being an immature adult are endless folks just buy one of these things. Plus it can order you pizza which is a bonus because then you don't even have to get up off the couch. Now if only our robot overlords and our saviour Jeff Bezos could make us a robot that would answer the door and bring the pizza to the couch...

title: Super Impressed!
body: The Amazon Firestick is the thing I never knew I needed. I'm a tech person and I have so many things in my ecosystem, including a Playstation 4, Nintendo Switch, Google Chromecast, and a Samsung Smart TV. By no means did I ever think I'd need another piece of tech to help bring my worlds together, but when Amazon had the Firestick on sale for Prime Day, I figured why not. Prior to owning the Firestick, I mostly managed my various TV streaming services (Hulu, Netflix, PS Vue, Prime videos, HBO, etc.) all through my Playstation 4 and my Samsung Smart TV.When I saw that I could access my PS Vue through the Amazon Firestick, I was SHOOK! I finally have one easy to use device that literally has every app I need for my streaming services and I no longer need to switch between my PS4 and Samsung Smart TV to be able to use all my services.It's super cool to be able to say something like, "Alexa, put on The Handmaids Tale" and it does exactly that for me. There are some shows that Alexa can't discern which app to open it in and it opens the wrong app sometimes (e.g. I ask to open Shark Tank from PS Vue for the latest episode, but instead it opens it in Hulu and plays Shark Tank season 1, episode 1), but I honestly don't really use the Alexa search function that often.. it's mostly just novelty for me.Alexa also has the ability to switch HDMI ports for me, so when I want to go back to my PS4 to play video games, I don't need to fetch my old TV remote to switch ports. "Alexa, switch to HDMI 1" and boom, it does it. "Alexa, switch to HDMI 3" and now I'm back on my Amazon Firestick. Granted, it works MOST of the time. Sometimes it doesn't register and it doesn't work and I have to use my old remote. I have to say I love only needing to use my Amazon Firestick remote in most cases, it's small and doesn't take up a lot of real estate. Calibrating it to my TV took seconds.My biggest issue with Amazon Firestick is that after 20 minutes of inactivity, it turns the screen off. I understand why this would be good, but this happens to me when I pause my TV show or go back to the home screen while I'm cooking, doing things around my apartment, working, etc. and it's annoying to have to turn it back on. I just wish they gave you an option to disable it from turning the screen off after a set amount of time.In conclusion, I love this thong and I would recommend it to anyone who has a host of streaming services and wants to have them ALL in one convenient place.

title: Darned Near Perfection
body: I've cut the cord. Still love Dish, just not the bill.The Amazon Recast, was the missing piece. With it, I can record, CBS, ABC, NBC, FOX, Me TV and watch the recorded shows on any of my 6 TV's with various forms of the Fire TV Devices.Not that Amazon needs my defending... but I feel the need to address some of the complaints by folks that are mostly not up to the task of using the product.1. Can't get the battery cover off the remote. I've experienced it.... used a single edged razor blade to start it.... and never had another problem. Heck I have to use pliers to get the Kraft salad dressing lids off... so I feel your pain.2. Buffering... If your using the free modem that your ISP gave you, you are asking for problems. I've got a Linksys 5400, better part of $300... but I've got about 75 wifi devices... no problem handing out a great stream of my Fiber Optic Internet.3. No Auio, No Video... you've got a defective TV or Fire Stick.... use another TV and a DVD/Blue Ray player to figure out which and replace. Hopefully it's the fire stick.4. Complaints that you have to pay for content you thought would be free. Locals... Fire Recast if antenna gets it. Direct TV Now if not.5. Complaints it won't run You Tube TV.... I can envision that could be frustrating. I envision Amazon will eventually support it, if it takes off... or I've looked at You Tube TV and all it's content IS available elsewhere.6. Over Priced? If there are other cheaper products that meet your needs buy them. The best way of getting Amazon to "hear" you is with your wallet.7. Didn't hook to my 3 receivers.... (volume)... sorry but it hooked brain dead easy to my 2 Pioneers and 1 Sony..8. In app interface funky.... I don't understand this one. I've used Netflix, Hulu, Amazon Prime and a couple of others on various devices... it seems as though the app interface is by service (Netflix, Hulu, Amazon Prime etc.) not by device ie Firestick, Roku, Apple TV. I could be wrong.9. Won't warrent the product.... there is a warranty period... they will for that time... or buy an extended warranty.I suppose I'm an Amazon Fanboy. 8 echo dots, 2 echo shows, an Echo Connect Amazon Recast.With Echo I can turn off or on every light in my house. Start my wife's car. Open the garage door. Turn on TV's get the news....A very enjoyable device.

title: Love It!
body: For years, I've heard people talk about the Amazon Fire Stick. Truthfully, I wasn't even clear on what they were or what they could do, just that it had something to do with TV. I never took the time to find out, until I found myself with an extra "dumb" TV that I had decided to set up in my bedroom. I didn't want to pay for an extra cable box, or rely solely on an antenna, so I decided to give the Fire Stick a try.After it arrived in only one day, I couldn't wait to get it set up and see what it could do. All, I can say is, WOW! I can't believe it took me this long to look into these. I never realized before that Smart TVs could not necessarily be updated to include the latest streaming apps. I discovered this when one of our relatively newer TVs could not access Disney+. Suddenly, I had an "obsolete" Smart TV. Crazy, right?After I got my Fire Stick set up, my mouth dropped open at all of the app options. I never even got around to watching anything the first couple of times I turned it on because there are so many options to explore! I love that it instantly learned and synced to my TV, without me having to tell it some code, so I can now control my TV with the Fire Stick remote. The Alexa voice control is also a bonus for when I know what I want to watch, but I'm not sure which app has it available. I can just tell Alexa to look for a show, and the search will show me all of the watching options for it.My absolute FAVORITE bonus, however, is the Bluetooth connectivity technology included with this Fire Stick. My TV sound is not the greatest, and I don't hear that well as it is. I am able to connect the sound to my Bluetooth ear buds and hear everything perfectly now! I can even pause the show directly from my ear buds. This is such a life saver, since now, I don't have to worry about disturbing the rest of the house at night with a loud TV.I will say that one evening, I was unable to watch anything, due to a slow connection speed, but that is the fault of my internet provider, not the Fire Stick. Every other time, there has been very little lag, and the picture quality is amazing.I am actually amazed at all I can do with this thing, given the price tag. WELL worth the money spent! We are looking forward to trying this out on our next camping trip, too.

title: Works great.
body: We had to get this because our older BD player is no longer supported by Netflix or Amazon Prime. As it turns out it was a nice upgrade. We still use the old BD player for playing DVDs but we can now use the Fire Stick for Netflix and Amazon Prime, as well as tons of other media apps that can also be installed on the FireStick, including YouTube, Amazon's Silk browser (web browser for FireStick and Kindle) and numerous other free or subscription TV, music, and movie apps. We don't use Alexa besides with FireStick. It is convenient and we hope that, as advertised, it is not listening except when you press the Alexa button on the FireStick remote. The voice interface is pretty much necessary in order to search for things because typing by selecting letters from an onscreen keyboard with the arrow keys gets old fast.Installation is pretty self-explanatory. You'll need your router password to connect up to WiFi. I guess the basic installation took me under an hour but you can spend several more hours figuring out which apps to install. The trick is to research the app before installing it because all the apps are free to install but some of them don't do anything useful unless you pay for a subscription, so a little advance research will save the trouble of installing and then uninstalling the ones you don't want.You'll also need to remember to switch the input on your TV so it gets input from the Fire Stick instead of from the DVD player or whatever other device you were using before. Once you do that, the FireStick remote does everything you need, including the TV volume, so you don't have to juggle two remotes. But if you watch a DVD then you have to go back to the DVD/TV remotes, or at least we do, based on the older equipment we have...The interface for Netflix with the FireStick is more like their web page, so more functional than the old TV interface but with the irritating habit of playing a trailer for whatever show you pause on for more than a second. I find that incredibly irritating but you can always mute it to reduce the distraction level. I wish they would let me decide when I want to play a trailer instead of forcing me to watch everything (grrr). That's my only grumble so far.

title: My TV on Vacation
body: Last November my wife and I went out of the country and I wanted to watch the Bears game. I have Dish Network, and the Dish Anywhere app on my phone, so I can watch it on my phone, but I couldn't figure out how to stream it to the TV in our hotel room. That was very frustrating.While researching this problem, I came across the Amazon Fire TV Stick. It said I could download the Dish Anywhere App and watch all my shows, both the live shows and the recorded ones. I ordered the Fire Stick in June and tried it out. Plugging it into the TV was simple, I just had to find the HDMI slot. I plugged the other end to the electrical outlet and used the remote to turn it on. It took awhile to download the updates, then I found the Dish Anywhere App and downloaded that. The nice thing is that everything is on the TV screen so you know what is going on, as opposed to the Google Chromecast that doesn't show up on the TV screen. Anyway, it took about a half hour to get everything downloaded. It was a bit longer than I expected, but once it's done you're good to go.Sure enough, I had my Dish Network shows, both live and recorded, at my disposal! Our hotel only had one premium channel, and my wife wanted to watch one of her shows that was on a different premium channel, and now it was not a problem. Further, I was able to watch the Chicago Cubs games.We don't watch a lot of TV on vacation, but certain shows are must-see for us.
